issues.title: How to globally access the JWT token from the request
issues.body:
I'm using a JWT authentication implementation as per the documentation: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt/

I can easily access the JWT token using DI in all my routers:

```
@router.get('/something')
async def some_function(token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)):
    # Some logic here
```

I'm trying to use SQLAlchemy's `before_compile` event to append some logic to all queries:

```
@event.listens_for(Query, 'before_compile', retval=True)
def sqlalchemy_before_compile(query: Query):
    # From here, how do I access the JWT token?
```

comments.author: Feijo
comments.body:
As a workaround I ended up going with [starlette-context](https://github.com/tomwojcik/starlette-context) + custom plugin.

Just wondering if there's another solution?

comments.author: valentin994
comments.body:
Do you mean accessing the JWT that the client sends you in his request?
If so you can add ```Authorize: AuthJWT = Depends()``` in your method, and 
then you just call ```Authorize.get_jwt_subject()```

comments.author: Feijo
comments.body:
Like this you mean?

```
@event.listens_for(Query, 'before_compile', retval=True)
def sqlalchemy_before_compile(query: Query, authorize=Depends()) -> Query:
    jwt = authorize.get_jwt_subject()
```

**`AttributeError: 'Depends' object has no attribute 'get_jwt_subject'`**

Bare in mind that this is not a FastAPI route, this is a SQLAlchemy event.

comments.author: valentin994
comments.body:
I meant in the route like this.

```
@router.get('/something')
async def some_function(Authorize: AuthJWT = Depends()):
    Authorize.jwt_required()
    what_you_used_to_create_the_token = Authorize.get_jwt_subject()
    function_to_use_that_subject(what_you_used_to_create_the_token)
```

So this looks if there is an access token cookie, if there is one the get_jwt_subject() will decode it back from what was used for the cookie to be made. For example, if you have a user register and login you would choose one unique identifier, let's say email, and then create a jwt with it, with this "something" route you would take the created cookies and get the email and then proceed to make database calls. Hope I didn't miss the point or your intent with the jwt.

comments.author: Feijo
comments.body:
Hi Valentin, thanks for trying to help but I'm afraid I'm trying to do something a bit unrelated.

I'm not trying to access the raw JWT token in one of my routes. There's a SQLAlchemy event I'm capturing, this event fires before a query compiles internally by sqlalchemy. The handler for this event is not in a request context, I cannot using `Depends`. I'm yes trying to capture the JWT, but the event handler has nothing to do with routes.

That's fine, as I said, starlette-context provides access to the current request from a global scope.

comments.body:
Thanks for the help here @valentin994 ! :clap: :bow: 

Thanks for reporting back and closing the issue @Feijo  :+1:

issues.title: Unable to get raw request body via Body route parameter
issues.body:
### Example

```Python
import uvicorn
from fastapi import Body, FastAPI

app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/test}")
async def update_item(
        payload: str = Body(...)
):
    return {}


uvicorn.run(app, host='127.0.0.1', port=8000)
```

```bash
curl -X POST "http://127.0.0.1:8000/test}" -H  "accept: application/json" -H  "Content-Type: application/text" -d "string"
```

Response: 422 - Unprocessable entity

### Description

Hi there!

I'm trying to pass a text body to my FastAPI endpoint. For sure I can do that [by accessing the request directly](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/using-request-directly/). However, from my perspective, the most intuitive way to do that is to use the `Body` like in the example below.

The problem is that Body parameter is always parsed (because of this fragment - [routing.py#L171](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/9e6b069c099d0b63df6e4c754497e0dffbffc5b5/fastapi/routing.py#L171)). How about changing that to something like:

```Python
            if body_field:
                if is_body_form:
                    body = await request.form()
                else:
                    body = await request.body()
                    if isinstance(body_field.type_, (dict, BaseModel)):
                        body = await request.json()
```

Currently `payload: str = Body(...)` always returns validation error, because JSON parsing would never produce a string, so this approach should be fully backward compatible. **I'd gladly create a PR for that if you think this option makes sense:)**

### Environment

* OS: macOS:
* FastAPI Version: 0.61.2
* Python version: 3.8.2

comments.author: ycd
comments.body:
What about accessing the raw `request body` from `Request` object?

```python
from fastapi import Request, FastAPI

@app.post("/dummypath")
async def get_body(request: Request):
    return await request.body()
```

comments.author: oryba
comments.body: @ycd yep, as I mentioned above, that's the way to get the raw body. I'm just thinking that it would be great to generalize Body to cover this as well - after all, it's a handy option that for some reason is limited to JSON now

comments.author: hvsummer
comments.body:
I saw that if we are using "await request.json()" it might be timeout if we transfer this object into backgroundtask.
how to get the json/raw body without await for it ? 
when the request come to the FastAPI, we should have it at running time, or at least have option to get it without await.

issues.title: OpenAPI generation failed with internal enums and with pydantic >=1.6.*
issues.body:
### First check

* [x] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
* [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.

### Example

```Python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from enum import Enum

app = FastAPI()


class EnumExample(Enum):
    a = 1


class Foo(BaseModel):
    class EnumExample(Enum):
        a = EnumExample.a
        b = 2

    x: EnumExample


class Bar(BaseModel):
    class EnumExample(Enum):
        a = EnumExample.a
        b = 2

    x: EnumExample


class TestResp(BaseModel):
    foo: Foo
    bar: Bar


@app.get("/", response_model=TestResp)
async def test():
    return TestResp(foo=Foo(x=Foo.EnumExample.a), bar=Bar(x=Bar.EnumExample.b))

```

### Description

<!-- Replace the content below with your own problem, question, or error -->

* Open the browser and call the endpoint `http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs`.
* It returns an error `Internal Server Error /openapi.json
`.
* The problem is relevant only with pydantic>=1.6.* versions

### StackTrace
```
  File "/Users/arxell/repo/mdcm/alfa/venv39/lib/python3.9/site-packages/fastapi/applications.py", line 143, in openapi
    return JSONResponse(self.openapi())
  File "/Users/arxell/repo/mdcm/alfa/venv39/lib/python3.9/site-packages/fastapi/applications.py", line 121, in openapi
    self.openapi_schema = get_openapi(
  File "/Users/arxell/repo/mdcm/alfa/venv39/lib/python3.9/site-packages/fastapi/openapi/utils.py", line 348, in get_openapi
    definitions = get_model_definitions(
  File "/Users/arxell/repo/mdcm/alfa/venv39/lib/python3.9/site-packages/fastapi/utils.py", line 31, in get_model_definitions
    model_name = model_name_map[model]
KeyError: <enum 'EnumExample'>
```

### Environment

* OS: macOS
* FastAPI: 0.62.0
* Python: 3.9.0
* pydantic: 1.7.3

comments.body:
> We had the same problem, updating to fastapi to 0.65 worked for us.

How do i update existing version of fastapi to latest in ubuntu. I'm getting some errors when using pip install fastapi

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body: Either uninstall it first, or use `pip install fastapi --upgrade`

comments.author: Sailokeshg
comments.body: That worked  Successfully installed fastapi-0.78.0 starlette-0.19.1 , but in http://127.0.0.1:8000/openapi.json . it is still showing version of fastapi as "0.1.0"

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
That is not the version of FastAPI, but the default version of your API itself. You can set the version like so:

`app = FastAPI(version='15.0.3')`

When not setting a version when initiating a `FastAPI()` application, it defaults to `0.1.0` which you will see in the docs. See also: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/metadata/?h=version#metadata-for-api

issues.title: Custom exception handling not updating OpenAPI status code
issues.body:
Hi, I'm trying to override the 422 status code to a 400 across every endpoint on my app, but it keeps showing as 422 on the openapi docs.

### Description
This is how i override the 422.

```Python
from fastapi import FastAPI, status
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder
from fastapi.exceptions import RequestValidationError
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse

app = FastAPI()

app.include_router(my_router, prefix="/log")


@app.exception_handler(RequestValidationError)
async def validation_exception_handler(request, exc):
    """
    Overrides FastAPI default status code for validation errors
    from 422 to 400
    """
    return JSONResponse(
        status_code=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST,
        content=jsonable_encoder({"detail": exc.errors(), "body": exc.body}),
    )

```
It returns 400 successfully but, the OpenAPI shows:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/29988404/100784915-ac3f6180-33ee-11eb-8b06-13b9b2b19ce9.png)

What's the correct way to override the 422 and update the OpenAPI docs schema?
Thank you
### Environment

* OS: **Linux**
* FastAPI Version: **0.61.1**

comments.author: Kludex
comments.body:
It's been some time since: #643 and #1376.
And actually no solution was provided on both issues (at least I didn't see it), so I think it's time.

Following the idea on: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/extending-openapi/?h=+swagger#override-the-method

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, status
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder
from fastapi.exceptions import RequestValidationError
from fastapi.openapi.utils import get_openapi
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()


class Potato(BaseModel):
    name: str
    color: str


@app.post("/items/")
def post_potato(potato: Potato):
    return potato


@app.get("/schemas")
def get_schemas():
    return app.openapi_schema


@app.exception_handler(RequestValidationError)
async def validation_exception_handler(request, exc):
    """Overrides FastAPI default status code for validation errors from 422 to 400."""
    return JSONResponse(
        status_code=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST,
        content=jsonable_encoder({"detail": exc.errors(), "body": exc.body}),
    )


def custom_openapi():
    if app.openapi_schema:
        return app.openapi_schema
    openapi_schema = get_openapi(
        title="Custom title",
        version="2.5.0",
        description="This is a very custom OpenAPI schema",
        routes=app.routes,
    )
    for path in openapi_schema["paths"]:
        for method in openapi_schema["paths"][path]:
            if openapi_schema["paths"][path][method]["responses"].get("422"):
                openapi_schema["paths"][path][method]["responses"][
                    "400"
                ] = openapi_schema["paths"][path][method]["responses"]["422"]
                openapi_schema["paths"][path][method]["responses"].pop("422")
    app.openapi_schema = openapi_schema
    return app.openapi_schema


app.openapi = custom_openapi
```

Images from swagger:
**Missing name!**
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7353520/100807180-293ffa80-3432-11eb-9daa-c2c734fd60df.png)

**Response!**
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7353520/100807227-4379d880-3432-11eb-9d67-c84ec579c34c.png)

**Documentation!**
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7353520/100807266-512f5e00-3432-11eb-87a0-160bf5e7896b.png)


Feel free to improve the given solution :smiley: 
Please close the issue if that satisfies you :+1:

issues.title: Moving questions from the issues to GitHub Discussions
issues.body:
(not following the issue template since this is not actually an issue regarding FastAPI)

So FastAPI has been maintaining a pretty active community over the past 2 years and I know from the past propositions to move community questions to Stack Overflow (#992, #2228) that the general concensus is that there are benefits in keeping these close to the git repo. It does have the downside of making the issues a fair bit more clogged than is convenient, and I know GitHub introduced a [forum-like "Discussions" tab](https://github.blog/2020-05-06-new-from-satellite-2020-github-codespaces-github-discussions-securing-code-in-private-repositories-and-more/#discussions) for repo owners to enable that has been [other repos](https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/discussions) have the kind of built-in forum we've been using the issues like for a while.

Would there be any interest in enabling this for the FastAPI repo so we can move new questions and feature discussions there?

issues.body:
Hi,
I am building a chat application where I wanna have a feature of sending files alongside with messages here is my code so far:

**main.py:**

```
@app.websocket("/api/user-chat")
async def chat(websocket: views.WebSocket):
    # get message sender
    sender = helper.get_current_user()
    try:
        while True:
            # get receiver and file information
            data = await websocket.receive_json()
            receiver = data['receiver']
            filename = data['filename']
            # get message file
            file = await websocket.receive_bytes()                
            file_dir = helper.gen_file_dir(sender, __file__)
            file_url = helper.create_file(file_dir, filename, file)
            data.update({
                    "file": file_url,
                    "filename": filename
             })                        
             await socket_manager.send_msg(data)
    except WebSocketDisconnect:
        socket_manager.disconnect(websocket, user)
        print("error occurred")
```

**helper.py**

```
# generate file url
def gen_file_dir(directory, file):
    root = pathlib.Path(file).parent.absolute()
    relative_path = "/static/media/uploads"
    absolute_path = str(root) + relative_path
    target_url = os.path.join(absolute_path, directory)    
    return target_url

# create file on disk
def create_file(dir, name, file):
    path = dir + "/" + name
    os.mkdir(dir)
    with open(path, "wb") as f:
        f.write(file)
    return path
```

comments.author: tiangolo
wiseaidev
comments.body: Hey everyone. The other day, I was working on a similar task of sending and receiving images between a javascript client, `react` in my case, and FastAPI. The only way that worked for me is by encoding the image into base64 at the client(you can look at [this example](https://github.com/brave-chat/brave-chat/blob/main/src/components/ChatFooter/index.js#L59) in case you are using react-dropzone) and then [decoding the image at the server side](https://github.com/brave-chat/brave-chat-server/blob/a7774c72748bd5c6de7ca66162de38d9a7f8239e/app/utils/pub_sub_handlers.py#L102-L104) with the base64 module. This way, you don't have to use the `receive_json` function as [receive_text](https://github.com/brave-chat/brave-chat-server/blob/a7774c72748bd5c6de7ca66162de38d9a7f8239e/app/utils/pub_sub_handlers.py#L84) would suffice. That's it, I hope this might help someone in the wild. Have a nice day/evening, fellas!

issues.title: Upload file gives body error with UploadFile
issues.body:
### Description
I am having issues uploading a file to a FastAPI server. I found similar issues:
#1367  and #405

However, there was no solution. The issue is that it works with small sized files, but when a file is bigger than 100Kb it does not work. It looks like the reading of the file does not finish before trying to run the other tasks like writing.

### Code sample
```
import uvicorn
from fastapi import FastAPI, File, UploadFile
from fastapi.responses import FileResponse
import aiofiles

app = FastAPI(title='Untitled', description='doThings', version='0.0.1')

uploads = 'uploads'
uploads_dir = pathlib.Path(os.getcwd(), uploads)

@app.post('/api/upload/')
async def upload_file(file: UploadFile=File(...)):
    """
    Upload the file to be processed.
    """
    print(file)
#    async with aiofiles.open(file.file, 'rb') as fin:
#       exfile = fin.read() 
    file_name = pathlib.Path(uploads_dir, file.filename)
    async with aiofiles.open(f'{file_name}', 'wb') as f:
        await f.write(await file.read())
    return {'filename': file.filename, 
                    'content-type': file.content_type}

if __name__ == '__main__':
    uvicorn.run('main:app', host='127.0.0.1', port=5003, log_level='info')
```
### Output

The error happens just when it tries to read the file. I have tried it only with the return and it does not work, neither. I have run many tests and I don't get where the error comes.

This is the output of the uvicorn server:

```

```

And in Postman I get this error message:

```
{

}
```

### Environment

OS: Windows 10
Python: Python 3.8.6
FastApi 0.61.2
Starlette 0.13.6

issues.title: How to send file with websockets
issues.body:
Hi,
I have already built a simple chatting application where connected users via websockets can send and receive messages in json format.

**Javascript:**
```
    function sendMessage() {
        var input = document.getElementById("messageText");
        data = {
            "message": input.value,
            "receiver": receiver
        };
        socket.send(JSON.stringify(data));
    }
```
**FastApi:**
```      
     while True:
            data = await websocket.receive_json()
            receiver = data["receiver"]
            if receiver:
                await socket_manager.to_specific_user(data)
```
Now I want to be able to send a file with websockets along with the message if any and store the file in the database what would be the better way ?

I tried this:

**Javascript:**
```
        data = {
            "message": input.value,
            "receiver": receiver,
            "file": file
        };           
```
**FastAPI**
```
            data = await websocket.receive_json()
            receiver = data["receiver"]
            file = data['file']
```
but that gives me error: `405 (Method Not Allowed)`

issues.title: Background Task - Kafka Consumer not getting closed on Application Shutdown Ctrl+C
issues.body:
I have added a Kafka Consumer in an API call and when I try to close the  application via Ctrl+C - I get below INFO message but the application never shuts down and I can see the PID attached to Kafka on port 9092 with uvicorn threads.
The impact on multiple times hitting of Ctrl_C also leads to nothing and my code for _shutdown event_ is never being called.

**^CINFO:     Shutting down
INFO:     Waiting for background tasks to complete. (CTRL+C to force quit)**

Below is my code - 
```

from fastapi import FastAPI, BackgroundTasks
from kafka import KafkaConsumer
from singleton import my_single_instance

consumer = KafkaConsumer('mitsuki',bootstrap_servers='localhost:9092',group_id = 'series-arc')

def consume_messages():
    for msg in consumer:
        print(msg)

app = FastAPI()

@app.on_event("shutdown")
def close_consumer():
    print('App Down')
    print(consumer.close())

@app.get('/start-consuming-kafka')
async def cool(bt:BackgroundTasks):
    if not my_single_instance.consumer_created:
        bt.add_task(consume_messages)
        my_single_instance.consumer_created = True
    return {'message':'Hello World 123'}
```

Background tasks are running even after hitting Ctrl+C. I waited for 30 mins to see the Kafka Consumer goes down but it never happened.

Is it the expected behavior or I have to go with some alternative approach.

My end goal is this - 

I want to add more tasks to increase the parallelism for same Kafka Consumer as I have multiple partitions. 
Rule for Kafka Consumer - Number of Partition = Number of Consumer with same Consumer group ID.

So I have 20 partitions for my Kafka Topic and I want to add 5 Background tasks.
I will spin up 4 replicas of same application instance in Kubernetes and then all 20 partitions will be covered up by background task consumer thread.

Kindly advise.

comments.author: n8sty
comments.body:
My guess is that this is caused by the `kafka` library you're using. The documentation for `KafkaConsumer.close` (https://kafka-python.readthedocs.io/en/master/apidoc/KafkaConsumer.html#kafka.KafkaConsumer.close) says "Close the consumer, waiting indefinitely for any needed cleanup" which makes me think that that call is waiting on "something" to happen before the call can be completed.

I'm also not sure this is the correct use of background tasks. The way I've used background tasks (sometimes with Kafka), is to have the task perform some action that takes longer than I want to wait in an HTTP route, but also doesn't need to run indefinitely. A Kafka consumer that's running perpetually could be run more effectively using https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-eventloop.html#asyncio.loop.run_in_executor that gets started during server startup. So, what's happening here, is because your background task is perpetual and therefore never completes, the consumer can never close. You can replicate this by replacing your `consume_messages` function with a long sleep. Once the sleep is finished, then the shutdown event will be triggered.

comments.author: arpan2501
comments.body:
Thanks @n8sty I will try to read more on _asyncio-eventloop_ but I found some unusual behavior -
I have stopped the application using **Ctrl+Z** and the application stopped but the PID were still active - 
How is it possible?
First Screenshot depicts application being closed but second screenshot says the PID is active. And next screenshot says Address already in use when I try to start the app again  - it's getting me bit confused now- 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20679587/99934079-f5563c80-2d82-11eb-9545-1b861628d072.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20679587/99934162-3b130500-2d83-11eb-9141-8b2e7bcda4d9.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20679587/99934313-aceb4e80-2d83-11eb-817d-8be06722a2d9.png)

comments.author: Mause
comments.body: Ctrl-C and Ctrl-Z do different things, C kills processes, Z turns them into a background process

comments.author: arpan2501
comments.body:
Thanks @Mause for quick reply and got it now.
But any thoughts on how can we stop the background task as it doesn't come in **shutdown event** when i do Ctrl+C. 
Or Background Task were not made for indefinitely running tasks like Kafka Consumer.

If there is _add_task_ then there should be _remove_task_ as well for BackgroundTasks class and they can be called up on **startup event** and **shutdown event** respectively.

Kindly advise.

comments.author: Mause
comments.body: Background tasks are very definitely not for indefinitely running talks. You will have to run it another way

comments.author: arpan2501
comments.body:
> Background tasks are very definitely not for indefinitely running talks. You will have to run it another way

It would be great to get some recommendation and I will definitely try it.

comments.body:

> A Kafka consumer that's running perpetually could be run more effectively using https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-eventloop.html#asyncio.loop.run_in_executor

comments.author: arpan2501
comments.body:
@Mause and @n8sty  - Thanks for the information and sharing the knowledge :-) . I have started on this track and currently my consumers(3 threads to 3 partitions - all different threads but same PID) is attached. Let me know your thoughts on the same if we can optimize it or some other technique to follow -

```

from fastapi import FastAPI, BackgroundTasks
from aiokafka import AIOKafkaConsumer
from singleton import my_single_instance
import asyncio

async def consume_messages():
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    consumer = AIOKafkaConsumer(
        'mitsuki',
        loop=loop, bootstrap_servers='localhost:9092',
        group_id="series-arc")
    await consumer.start()
    try:
        async for msg in consumer:
            print("consumed: ", msg.topic, msg.partition, msg.offset,
                  msg.key, msg.value, msg.timestamp)
    finally:
        print('Signal to close')
        await consumer.stop()

app = FastAPI()

@app.get('/cool')
async def start(bt:BackgroundTasks):
    if not my_single_instance.consumer_created:
        #bt.add_task(consume_messages)  Commented this Background task and now Ctrl + C works
        asyncio.ensure_future(consume_messages())
        asyncio.ensure_future(consume_messages())
        asyncio.ensure_future(consume_messages())
        my_single_instance.consumer_created = True
    return {'message':'Ok'}
```

comments.author: n8sty
comments.body:
The above still isn't the correct use of background tasks. Background tasks shouldn't run for the lifetime of an application, they should be used for tasks that need to happen asynchronously to a HTTP request. Like I said above, try using some other method to create and then safely stop an additional process. One such way is `asyncio.loop.run_in_executor`, but there are plenty of others (eg: threads, processes, cron). If you want to use an HTTP request to control a longer running background process you can have the HTTP request synchronously set some flag on `app.state`, and the background process can act according to the value(s) of that flag. I'll repeat just to emphasize, background tasks are meant to be ephemeral and should not run for the lifetime of a FastAPI application (or Starlette) instance.

I think this issue should be closed since there's no actual FastAPI specific component to the question.

comments.author: arpan2501
comments.body:
Thanks All, I will be closing the issue which clarifies the background tasks are not for _forever_ running tasks.

Anyone else having the same query w.r.t Kafka Consumer in Web App can use the below process(No More Background tasks). 
```

@app.on_event("startup")
async def app_start_event():
    print('Hello')
    asyncio.ensure_future(consume_messages())

@app.get('/cool')
async def start():
    return {'message':'Ok'}
```

and you can start the application with 3 workers - 
`uvicorn main:app --workers 3`

So three threads will be spawned up each being attached to Kafka Partition.
Cheers to @Mause and @n8sty for clarifying.

issues.title: Response model doesn't allow models with allow_arbitrary_types
issues.body:
### First check

* [x] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
* [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
* [x] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
* [x] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
* [x] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

<!-- 

I'm asking all this because answering questions and solving problems in GitHub issues consumes a lot of time. I end up not being able to add new features, fix bugs, review Pull Requests, etc. as fast as I wish because I have to spend too much time handling issues.

All that, on top of all the incredible help provided by a bunch of community members that give a lot of their time to come here and help others.

That's a lot of work they are doing, but if more FastAPI users came to help others like them just a little bit more, it would be much less effort for them (and you and me 😅).

-->

### Example - not specifying `response_model`

Creating a `Pydantic` model that has a field with an arbitrary class will raise `RuntimeError` stating that there is no validator for class `ArbitraryClass`:

```Python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()


class ArbitraryClass:

    def __init__(self, attr: str) -> None:
        self.attr = attr


class SomeModel(BaseModel):

    name: str = 'SomeModel'
    arbitrary_field: ArbitraryClass

    class Config:
        arbitrary_types_allowed = True


@app.get('/')
async def return_model_with_arbitrary_type():
    model = SomeModel(arbitrary_field=ArbitraryClass('attr_value'))
    return model
```

### Results of running example without `response_model` specified

This works fine, since `Pydantic` allows creation of models where fields have arbitrary types when we use `arbitrary_types_allowed` in `Config`. The returned JSON is also nicely formatted, as`attribute: value` pair:

```json
{
  "name": "SomeModel",
  "arbitrary_field": {
    "attr": "attr_value"
  }
}
```

### Example - specifying `response_model`

When we try to specify that the response model for this endpoint is `SomeModel`:

```Python
@app.get('/', response_model=SomeModel)
async def return_model_with_arbitrary_type():
    model = SomeModel(arbitrary_field=ArbitraryClass('attr_value'))
    return model
```

We get an error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/fisheye36/Projects/fastapi-issue/.env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/utils.py", line 65, in create_response_field
    return response_field(field_info=field_info)
  File "pydantic/fields.py", line 284, in pydantic.fields.ModelField.__init__
  File "pydantic/fields.py", line 362, in pydantic.fields.ModelField.prepare
  File "pydantic/fields.py", line 538, in pydantic.fields.ModelField.populate_validators
  File "pydantic/validators.py", line 629, in find_validators
RuntimeError: no validator found for <class 'backend.main.ArbitraryClass'>, see `arbitrary_types_allowed` in Config

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/Library/Frameworks/Python3.framework/Versions/3.8/lib/python3.8/runpy.py", line 193, in _run_module_as_main
    return _run_code(code, main_globals, None,
  File "/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/Library/Frameworks/Python3.framework/Versions/3.8/lib/python3.8/runpy.py", line 86, in _run_code
    exec(code, run_globals)
  File "/Users/fisheye36/Projects/fastapi-issue/src/backend/__main__.py", line 1, in <module>
    from backend.main import run_server
  File "/Users/fisheye36/Projects/fastapi-issue/src/backend/main.py", line 37, in <module>
    async def return_model_with_arbitrary_type():
  File "/Users/fisheye36/Projects/fastapi-issue/.env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/routing.py", line 494, in decorator
    self.add_api_route(
  File "/Users/fisheye36/Projects/fastapi-issue/.env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/routing.py", line 439, in add_api_route
    route = route_class(
  File "/Users/fisheye36/Projects/fastapi-issue/.env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/routing.py", line 315, in __init__
    ] = create_cloned_field(self.response_field)
  File "/Users/fisheye36/Projects/fastapi-issue/.env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/utils.py", line 91, in create_cloned_field
    use_type.__fields__[f.name] = create_cloned_field(
  File "/Users/fisheye36/Projects/fastapi-issue/.env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/utils.py", line 94, in create_cloned_field
    new_field = create_response_field(name=field.name, type_=use_type)
  File "/Users/fisheye36/Projects/fastapi-issue/.env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/utils.py", line 67, in create_response_field
    raise fastapi.exceptions.FastAPIError(
fastapi.exceptions.FastAPIError: Invalid args for response field! Hint: check that <class 'backend.main.ArbitraryClass'> is a valid pydantic field type
```

### Question

`Pydantic` allows models like that. `FastAPI` also allows returning models like that and handles them well. The only problem here is something wrong happens when that kind of model is specified as a response model.

I know I could create validators for `ArbitraryClass`, but what if the class is coming from some other library and I can't edit its source?

### Environment

* OS: macOS Catalina 10.15.7
* FastAPI Version: 0.61.2
* Python version: 3.8.2

@Nemesis7, I set the `arbitrary_types_allowed` option globally as follows, which worked for me. I haven't tried @pmsoltani's approach though.

issues.title: Dependency injection without resorting to global state
issues.body:
Hi there! I'm starting the FastAPI web server via Uvicorn after completing various initialization steps (reading command line arguments, reading a configuration file, setting up shared memory etc.). Now I would like to access said dependencies (command line arguments, config, shared memory, …) in my endpoints or, more specifically, pass them as argument to my endpoints. Unfortunately, the current way of injecting dependencies into endpoints via `Depends` basically amounts to using global state, as the dependency has to be declared in the endpoint's function signature as a *Callable* and so has to be available already when Python runs the semantic analysis on the code.

At the same time, `app.include_router(my_router, additional_dependencies=[ ])` does not solve this issue, either, as AFAIK there is no way to access these dependencies inside my endpoints.

What's the best practice to solve this issue? Ideally I would like to do something like

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass

from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter

@dataclass
class MyConfig:
    foo: str = ""

router = APIRouter()

@router.post("/")
def my_endpoint(my_config: MyConfig = DependsOnType(MyConfig)):
    ...

def create_app(config: MyConfig) -> FastAPI:
    app = FastAPI()
    app.register_dependency(type=MyConfig, value=config)
    app.include_router(router, prefix="/myrouter")
    return app
```

Then I could do `app = create_app(MyConfig(foo="bar"))` to create different versions of the app, depending on the configuration and other dependencies.

EDIT: While there is a way to achieve this (see Kludex's comment below), it has various deficiencies (see the discussion below) and I would like to propose the above functionality involving `DependsOnType` and `register_dependency()` as a new feature.

comments.author: Kludex
comments.body:
The last two sections can help you: https://www.starlette.io/applications/#storing-state-on-the-app-instance

Store in `app.state`, and get from `request.app`.

comments.author: codethief
comments.body:
Ohh, thank you so much!

I think my suggestion from above involving `DependsOnType()` would still be desirable, though, because monkey-patching `app.state`…

- breaks type hints as the type of `app.state` is unclear to endpoints.
- it makes all dependencies available to *all* endpoints which might be dangerous.
- it hides an endpoint's dependencies because the module containing the endpoint does not even need to import the required type.

I have therefore converted my question into a feature request.

comments.author: Mause
comments.body:
In essence you want a singleton, which isn't currently supported by the dependency injection system in fastapi.

I wrote a quick implementation for my own system though, you're welcome to use it: https://github.com/Mause/media/blob/master/rarbg_local/singleton.py#L33

comments.author: codethief
comments.body:
I don't want a singleton, at least not in the strict sense. I just want to pass an instance of an arbitrary class (whether it's a singleton class or not) to all the endpoints in my FastAPI app.

As for your implementation, could you give an example on how to use your code?

comments.author: Mause
codethief
comments.body:
Ok, looks like we were talking about different things here. A singleton to me is a class of which only one instance *can* exist. Of course there are many classes of which there only is one single instance at a given time but these are not necessarily singletons. Then again, this is only a matter of connotation.

In any case, thank you for your example! However, it doesn't really help me with my issue because I want `get_settings()` to return a different `Settings` object, depending on the chosen runtime profile etc. and for that I need proper dependency injection.

(Side remark: Why didn't you simply use a [@cache](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.cache) decorator to avoid recreating the `Settings` instance?)

comments.author: Mause
comments.body:
Yep, different meanings for different implementations (this is similar to spring boot's meaning of singleton).

Maybe you could expand what you mean by runtime profile?

I didn't use `@cache` as that would cache it globally within the python interpreter - this implementation essentially caches it per `FastAPI` instance, so if you create a new `FastAPI` instance per test, your tests won't leak state (for example).

comments.author: codethief
comments.body:
> I didn't use @cache as that would cache it globally within the python interpreter - this implementation essentially caches it per FastAPI instance, so if you create a new FastAPI instance per test, your tests won't leak state (for example).

Oh, I see. That makes sense! :)

> Maybe you could expand what you mean by runtime profile?

In my particular case, the FastAPI process gets started by a parent process which starts the whole application stack. This parent process loads a configuration file, among others, and converts it into an instance of a certain `Configuration` class, which it then passes on to the web server via the `args` argument in `multiprocessing.Process()` like so:

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from multiprocessing import Process

from fastapi import FastAPI
import uvicorn

@dataclass
class Configuration:
    host: str
    port: int
    # …

# This function is invoked by the parent process
def start_webserver_process(config: Configuration):
    process = Process(
        target=webserver_process,
        name="Webserver process",
        args=(config, ),
    )
    process.start()

def webserver_process(config: Configuration):
    app = FastAPI()
    app.include_router(...)

    # Inject config object since some endpoints need it
    app.state.config = config

    uvicorn.run(
        app, host=config.host, port=config.port,
    )
```

As you can see, I cannot define a `get_settings()` singleton at "compilation" time as the configuration won't be known until runtime. I also don't want to set the config as module-global state in my web server process, as the state would leak during automated tests.

comments.author: Mause
comments.body:
You could follow a similar pattern as my singleton function, like so:

```python


def get_configuration() -> Configuration:
    raise NotImplementedError("Configuration not injected")


def webserver_process(config: Configuration):
    ...

    app.dependency_overrides[get_configuration] = lambda: config

    ....
```

This lets you keep type safety as well

comments.author: codethief
comments.body:
Yeah, I ended up doing something very similar:

```python
from typing import Type, TypeVar

from fastapi import FastAPI


T = TypeVar("T")


def inject_dependency(app: FastAPI, type_: Type[T], value: T):
    if not hasattr(app.state, "dependencies"):
        app.state.dependencies = {}

    if not isinstance(value, type_):
        raise ValueError("value needs to be an instance of type_")

    app.state.dependencies[type_] = value


def get_dependency(app: FastAPI, type_: Type[T]) -> T:
    value = app.state.dependencies[type_]
    if not isinstance(value, type_):
        raise ValueError(
            "The dependency that was found under the key type_ is not an instance of "
            "type_"
        )
    return value
```

Usage:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

app = FastAPI()
config = Configuration()
inject_dependency(app, Configuration, config)

@app.get("/")
def get_device_state_endpoint(http_request: Request):
    config = get_dependency(http_request.app, Configuration)
    # …
```

Still, I think this should be an official feature.

comments.author: Mause
comments.body:
With the above snippet, you can just inject it as a normal dependency (and you don't really need the function either)

```python

def webserver_process(config: Configuration):
    ...

    app.dependency_overrides[Configuration] = lambda: config

    ....


@app.get('/')
def index(configuration: Configuration = Depends()):
    ...

```

This is a working example:
```python

from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends
from pydantic import BaseModel


class Config(BaseModel):
    name: str


app = FastAPI()
app.dependency_overrides[Config] = lambda: Config(name='hello')


@app.get('/')
def index(config: Config = Depends()):
    return config


tc = TestClient(app)
print(tc.get('/').json())

```

comments.author: gcasar
comments.body:
@Mause this approach would inject the class as the API body  - Thats the main difference between your answer and what I understand @codethief was trying to achieve.

Is more similar to what FastAPI does for `Request` and the db session from the examples. Its not part of the API spec, its used to get the data / do business logic. As it stands the two are intertwined and you need to have a class that is not serializable.

comments.author: jonathanslenders
comments.body:
I worked around this limitation by using Python's context variables.

A few lines of middleware will put them in place. The API endpoints can then either use some helper functions to get data from the context variables, possibly through ``fastapi.Depends``.

It's possible for the entry point of the application, e.g., the ``main()`` to wrap the ``ASGI`` app in middleware like this:
```python
from typing import Optional
from contextvars import ContextVar
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

class ServerState:
    " Data to be exposed. Whatever would be global state otherwise. "

_server_state_var: ContextVar[Optional[ServerState]] = ContextVar("_server_state_var")

class ContextvarMiddleware:
    """
    Middleware that exposes the `ServerState` through contextvars to all
    underlying API endpoints.
    """
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, server_state: ServerState) -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.server_state = server_state

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        _server_state_var.set(self.server_state)

        try:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
        finally:
            _server_state_var.set(None)

def create_api_handler(server_state: ServerState) -> ASGIApp:
    # To be called from "main()" or wherever the state is set.
    return ContextvarMiddleware(app, server_state=server_state)
```

With this, it's easy to define the ``FastAPI`` instance at the top level of a module and have it decoupled from any mutable state:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends

def get_server_state() -> ServerState:
    server_state = _server_state_var.get()
    if server_state is None:
        raise RuntimeError("Application not wrapped in ContextvarMiddleware.")
    return server_state

api = FastAPI()

@api.get('/')
def home(server_state: ServerState = Depends(get_server_state)):
   ...
```

Personally, I prefer to run ASGI applications using Hypercorn for this, because it allows running ASGI applications programatically from a ``main()`` function, which is what you need if you have an installable entry point (with ``setup.py`` and maybe ``click`` for command line parsing).

@codethief: Would the above work for you?
@tiangolo: What do you think about this? Would it be possible or make sense to add this to the docs somewhere? (I don't think it's too much boilerplate to copy/paste for the people that need this functionality. Those who need it probably want to change it anyway, and will appreciate that it can be completely typed (which is maybe not the case if we try to come up with a generic solution.)

comments.author: codethief
comments.body:
Hi @jonathanslenders , thanks so much for this interesting proposal!

To be honest, this is the first time I'm hearing of Python's contextvars module. Having now read the corresponding [PEP 567](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/), though, I'm getting the impression it's mainly meant for context-dependent state, i.e. state that's supposed to vary from execution context to execution context. In my case, however, I want to do the exact opposite: Namely, I'd like to inject the *same* global object / the same singleton into every execution context without having to actually resort to global state or the singleton pattern because it would obscure my application's true dependencies. While this would certainly work with contextvars, it requires the var to be set for every execution context separately which seems like a bit of overkill and, in case this is of any relevance, not very elegant.

PS: I believe in 

```python
    return ContextvarMiddleware(app, server_state=server_state)
```

you meant to write `self.app`?

comments.author: poolpitako
comments.body:
is there any conclusion to this ticket?
What's the pattern recommended for injections outside of the request/app scope?

issues.title: Is middleware setup done on a different context than the one running the requests?
issues.body:
### First check

* [x ] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
* [x ] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
* [x ] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
* [x ] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
* [x ] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
* [x ] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
* [x ] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
* [x ] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).
* [x ] After submitting this, I commit to one of:
    * Read open issues with questions until I find 2 issues where I can help someone and add a comment to help there.
    * I already hit the "watch" button in this repository to receive notifications and I commit to help at least 2 people that ask questions in the future.
    * Implement a Pull Request for a confirmed bug.

### Example

Example taken directly from [aio_nameko_proxy](https://github.com/laiyongtao/aio_nameko_proxy) (just removed innecesary parts)

```Python
from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket
from pydantic import BaseSettings

from aio_nameko_proxy.wrappers import FastApiNamekoProxyMiddleware, rpc_cluster  # contextvars required in py36


class Settings(BaseSettings):
    # AMQP_URI: Required
    NAMEKO_AMQP_URI = "pyamqp://guest:guest@127.0.0.1:5672"
    # rpc_exchange
    NAMEKO_RPC_EXCHANGE = "nameko-rpc"


settings = Settings()

app = FastAPI()

app.add_middleware(FastApiNamekoProxyMiddleware, config=settings)

@app.get("/")
async def test():    
    rpc = await rpc_cluster.get_proxy()

    result = await rpc.rpc_demo_service.normal_rpc("demo")

    return {"hello": "world"}

```

### Description

* Open the browser and call the endpoint `/`.
* It raises `RuntimeError("Please initialize your cluster before using")`.

This is because a ContextVar used for storing the cluster is returning None even though it is being correctly setup at startup, this has me wondering if some different context is being used when "initializing" middleware and it's not copied over when running the actual requests.

### Environment

* OS: Ubuntu 20.04 using Docker python 3.8-slim
* FastAPI Version: 0.61.2
* aio-nameko-proxy version: 1.3.1


### Additional context

Related ticket on laiyongtao/aio_nameko_proxy#3

comments.author: Mause
comments.body: This looks like a bug in the library you're using, not in fastapi?

comments.author: alfaro28
comments.body: probably is, but my question is why don't the ContextVars work on fastapi?

comments.author: Mause
comments.body: They do work, but the library is using them incorrectly

comments.author: alfaro28
comments.body: could you please elaborate?

comments.author: Mause
comments.body: The library is initialising the cluster in the main thread, rather than in the task that is processing the request: https://github.com/laiyongtao/aio_nameko_proxy/blob/master/aio_nameko_proxy/wrappers/fastapi.py#L80

comments.author: alfaro28
comments.body: I think the intention is to use a single instance of the cluster and not create one per request (to my understanding that would be more efficient), is that posible? or even desirable?, the cluster has a connection pool, but what is the point of using a pool if you are going to create a pool per request?

comments.author: Mause
comments.body: If the intention is to create only a single pool, then why is it being stored in a context var at all? Looking at the project, there aren't any tests for that middleware proving that it ever worked, so I suggest you follow up with them

comments.author: alfaro28
comments.body: could you recommend an approach on how to use the single pool? and what is your opinion on having a pool per request vs a "global" pool?, sorry I'm still very new to async frameworks and I'm a little confused about this topic

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
This could be releated to the fact that previously contextvars were not propagated to thread workers, but that's fixed in AnyIO here: https://github.com/agronholm/anyio/pull/390, part of AnyIO `3.4.0`, used by FastAPI.

If you upgrade your FastAPI and AnyIO version it might just work now.

issues.title: How to reach the template directory from a router.py in a sub diretory?
issues.body:
I tried to follow the tutoria to form a bigger application
https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/bigger-applications/
the project structure is like :
```
.
├── app
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── main.py
│   ├── model.py
│   ├── countries.gz
│   └── routers
│         ├── __init__.py
│         ├── items.py
│         └── users.py
│   └── templates
│         ├── layout.html
│         ├── home.html
│         └── world.html
```
main.py:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, APIRouter
from .routers import us_router, world_router

app = FastAPI()
# should I add some config here?

app.include_router(us_router.router)
app.include_router(world_router.wrouter)

```

in the /routers/items.py
```python
from fastapi.templating import Jinja2Templates

wrouter = APIRouter()

templates = Jinja2Templates(directory='templates')
# if the router.py is in the same level of templates directory, this will work.
# should I change something here? I tried '../templates', not work.

@wrouter.get("/world", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def world(request: Request):
    flist = fig.fig_world('total_cases')
    return templates.TemplateResponse('world.html', {'request': request,
                                                     'line_latest': flist[0],
                                                     }
                                      )

```
The problem:
cannot find the world.html, which is in templates directory.
If I use a absolute directory 'home/eric/fastapi/app/templates', it will work.

model.py:
```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_pickle('countries.gz')
# this is very confusing, I tried './countries.gz', not work

......

```
Problem:
cannot find the 'countries.gz' in the same directory. use absolute directory will work.

expected answer:
As a beginner, I am confused on the logic. I would like to know what is the best practice to handle the directories in the fastapi project?

Thanks a lot.

comments.body:
can you put here your executes command?
do you run the command inside `app` directory or outside of that?

comments.author: waterchinap
comments.body:
I execute just outside the app directory: uvicorn app.main:app --reload

OH!
change like this will work:
```
templates = Jinja2Templates(directory='./app/templates')
```

Thank you so much, the project root dir is the excute directory, NOT the main.py.directory.

My follow up question is:
Will it be more reasonable if I set the main.py directory as root? where should I config, If I want to do this,

comments.author: Mause
comments.body:
You probably want to do something like this:


```python
from os.path import dirname, join

current_dir = dirname(__file__)  # this will be the location of the current .py file
templates = join(current_dir, 'templates')
```

issues.title: Bug: Breaking change in new release of Starlette
issues.body: Yesterday, [Starlette 0.14.1](https://github.com/encode/starlette/releases/tag/0.14.1) was released, which removes `UJSONResponse`. As a result, https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/b53c443a06f898189e0f458dd1d7cbe55742192b/fastapi/responses.py#L10 breaks on start-up.

comments.body:
> What do you mean by "dotenv syntax"?

Hi I have now both versions to working

With Pydantic BaseSetting:
```
# pydantic config file
class DatabaseSettings(BaseSettings):
    DB_URL: str
    DB_NAME: str

    class Config:
        env_file = '.env'
        env_file_encoding = 'utf-8'

# main.py
@app.on_event("startup")

    app.mongodb_client = AsyncIOMotorClient(settings.DB_URL)
    app.mongodb = app.mongodb_client[settings.DB_NAME]
    
```

With dotenv syntax:
```
# main.py
@app.on_event("startup")
async def startup_db_client():
    # Instead of Pydantic BaseSettings use .env
    app.mongodb_client = AsyncIOMotorClient(os.getenv("DB_URL"))
    app.mongodb = app.mongodb_client[os.getenv("DB_NAME")]
```

All good on my end for now...

comments.author: hellocoder2020
comments.body:
It depends on where you place your .env file at bro.
If you place .env in root, so just load `env_file = '.env'`
If you place .env nested directory, e.g 

`├── app`
`│   ├── config.py`
`│   ├── .env`

So just `env_file = 'app/.env'`


[Checkout More](https://youtu.be/2DfaD595Wi0)

issues.title: Performance problem wit streaming response
issues.body:
Hi I noticed slow response using StreamingResponse.

compared to Flask it is much more slower.

Flask code runnig under flask server:
```
from flask import Flask, Response

app = Flask("app")

@app.route("/")
def stream():
    def generate():
        while True:
            yield "test string\r\n"

    return Response(generate(), headers={"Content-Type": "text/plain"})

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=60580)
```
testing with curl I get:
```
  % Total    % Received % Xferd  Average Speed   Time    Time     Time  Current
                                 Dload  Upload   Total   Spent    Left  Speed
100 10.0M    0 10.0M    0     0   419k      0 --:--:--  0:00:24 --:--:--  380
```
FastAPI code running under uvicorn:

```
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse


app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
def stream():
    def generate():
        while True:
            yield "test  string\r\n"

    response = StreamingResponse(generate(), media_type="text/plain")

    return response
```
I get
```
  % Total    % Received % Xferd  Average Speed   Time    Time     Time  Current
                                 Dload  Upload   Total   Spent    Left  Speed
100 1203k    0 1203k    0     0  35558      0 --:--:--  0:00:34 --:--:-- 37239
```
you can compare Dload speed which is very slow comapred to Flask.

comments.author: Enormoss
comments.body:
Environment is the same, Python3.8, CentOS Linux release 8.1.1911 (Core)

FastAPI 0.61.1
Uvicorn 0.12.2
starlette 0.13.6

comments.author: ycd
comments.body:
Can you try comparing a fixed amount?

Compare this with an equal Flask Code to this. 

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

app = FastAPI()


async def test_string_generator():
    for _ in range(100000):
        yield "test  string\r\n"


@app.get("/")
async def main():
    return StreamingResponse(test_string_generator())
```

```zsh
time curl --silent --output /dev/null http://127.0.0.1:8000

Out: 0,00s user 0,01s system 35% cpu 0,043 total
```

comments.author: Enormoss
comments.body:
Amazing! That's it, now it is  a little faster than Flask and even CPU loads drops.
Thank you

comments.author: scriptator
comments.body:
> Can you try comparing a fixed amount?

According to my investigations with that code, what makes the real difference is making the generator function `async`. Omit that and the example takes 7 seconds instead of 0.1 seconds on my machine. The reason is probably that StreamingResponse can only handle async iterators and if you pass in a non-async one it needs to offload that to a thread pool which seems to have quite some overhead.

comments.author: iashraful
comments.body: **Moral of the issue:** We must use async generator/iterator to get the best performance. I was stuck a day with same kind of problem. Finally, I rewrite the my small chunk of code with async generator and it worked like champ. ❤️

issues.title: How to access user credentials send through ajax call with OAuth2PasswordRequestForm
issues.body:
Hi,

I am creating a simple login form on the frontend and sending the username and password to my endpoint with ajax call like this:

**Ajax call to endpoint:**
```
$.ajax({
       type: 'POST',
       url: '/api/token',
       contentType: "application/json",
       dataType: "json",
       data: JSON.stringify( {"username": $("#username").val(), "password": $("#password").val()})
});
```
on the backend I want to receive the credentials and generate a token after successfully login the user like this:

**My endpoint**

```
@app.post("/api/token")
async def login(response: Response, credentials: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm = Depends(), db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    user = views.authenticate(db, credentials.username, credentials.password)
    if not user:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Incorrect username or password")
    access_token = views.gen_token(user.username)
    response.set_cookie(key="access_token", value=f"Bearer {access_token}", httponly=True)
    return
```

with the above code I get error:  `422 Unprocessable Entity` so I replaced  `OAuth2PasswordRequestForm` with my custom pydantic model:

**Login validator:**

```
class LoginValidator(BaseModel):
    username: str
    password: str
```

Now it works but then this line: `response.set_cookie(key="access_token", value=f"Bearer {access_token}", httponly=True)` does not set the cookie and hence the user cannot be logged in. 

**Edit:**

Okay I saw this in docs:

> The spec also states that the username and password must be sent as form data (so, no JSON here).

so I modified ajax call to this:

```
$.ajax({
       type: 'POST',
       url: '/api/token',
       data:{"username": $("#username").val(), "password": $("#password").val()}
});
```

Okay now I don't get error by the cookie is still not set.

issues.title: Mutliple OpenAPI Spec Endpoints on One App
issues.body:
I am working on an application that has quite a large number of endpoints. I have been using tags to group sets of API however the APIs themselves are quite different so I was wondering if there is a way of hosting multiple OpenAPI endoints.

comments.body: thanks for this. Will look later on in the week and let you know how i go

comments.author: luebke-dev
comments.body:
you can create two FastAPI apps and mount them in starlette
```
from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette.applications import Starlette
from starlette.routing import Mount

api1 = FastAPI(title="API1")
api2 = FastAPI(title="API2")

@api1.get("/test")
def test_api1():
    return "API1"

@api2.get("/test")
def test_api2():
    return "API2"

app = Starlette(routes=[
    Mount("/api1", api1),
    Mount("/api2", api2)
])
```

comments.author: includeamin
comments.body:
there are many solutions for that.
for example.
1- the first, token should remove from the client-side.
2- add token to  Blacklist that store in DB ( better to use Redis for better Performance ) with `TTL== Expiration time of token`.
so before token expiration, all requests with that token will ignored or blocked and after TTL or expiration of token. all requests with that token, of course, will be ignored because of expiration.

comments.author: ArcLightSlavik

If your building from the ground up and require strict logout then it's better to use cookie auth instead of jwt. 
If you are stuck with jwt you can create a Blacklist with TTL (redis is a very good choice) after which you make middleware which checks if the token is in the Blacklist, if it is it returns a 401.

comments.author: caeser1996
comments.body:
> 

The logout functionality can be implemented by providing an endpoint that invalidates the current access token, effectively logging the user out.

Here's an example implementation in FastAPI:
```
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, Header
from app.configurations.oauth2 import oauth2_scheme

routes = APIRouter(prefix="/user", tags=["Authentication"])

@routes.post("/logout")
def user_logout(Authorization: str = Header(None)):
    oauth2_scheme.revoke_token(Authorization)
    return {"message": "Token revoked"}

```
In this example, a /user/logout endpoint is created that takes in an Authorization header, which contains the access token. The revoke_token method from your oauth2_scheme is called to invalidate the token. This function should be implemented to actually store the revoked tokens and invalidate them in future requests.

It's also important to note that logging out in an OAuth2-based system only invalidates the current access token, not the refresh token. To fully log out the user, you'll need to invalidate both tokens.

issues.body:
I'm using the dependency injection mechanism to define common parameters for my requests. my common params model uses a validator that makes sure only one of the boolean flags supplied is true, not both - 

```python

class CommonParams(BaseModel):
    test_id: UUID
    earliest: Optional[datetime]
    latest: Optional[datetime]
    filters: Optional[FiltersInput]
    overwrite: bool = False
    ignore_cache: bool = False

    @validator('ignore_cache')
    def validate_overwrite_or_ignore(cls, v, values):
        assert not (v or values['overwrite']), 'Either "overwrite" or "ignore_cache" can be True, not both!'


@calculate_router.post("/{test_id}")
async def calculate(commons: CommonParams = Depends()):
    return "this should not be printed"
```

As far as i understand, if my request receives true for both "overwrite" and "ignore cache" params, request should fail with a 422 validation error. But instead im receiving a 500 with the validation error printed as log.

Is that intended or am i doing something wrong?

comments.author: SirTelemak
comments.body:
I guess Depends works bad with pydantic's models
I think it because we want Depends to work with any Callable, not only with BaseModel. So FastAPI parses `__init__` params from dependency, validates them and after that transfer back to Depends callable where validation exception happens

alternatively, 
- you can use `HTTPException` inside the validator with 422 (`HTTP_422_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY`or etc) status code.
- or put CommonParams in the Body.

issues.title: @app.middleware("http")
issues.body:
@app.middleware("http")

I quote middleware to calculate the process time, while I find the requests per second (RPS) to be 60% of without middleware. That means 4000rps to around 2500rps. Is it right? 
Could you please give some advice to improve the performance of middleware?

```
@app.middleware("http")
async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
    start_timestamp = time.time()
    request.state.timestamp = start_timestamp
    response = await call_next(request)
    process_time = time.time() - start_timestamp
    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
    logger.info({"status_code": response.status_code,
                 "url": request.url,
                 "client": request.client.host,
                 "timestamp": start_timestamp,
                 "process_time": process_time})
    return response
```

comments.author: Kludex
comments.body:
It's a known issue on Starlette side.

When you create a middleware using the decorator `@app.middleware('http')` it creates a [BaseHTTPMiddleware](https://www.starlette.io/middleware/#basehttpmiddleware) based middleware. Precisely the class that comes with the issue (https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/919). 

You should create a pure [ASGI middleware](https://asgi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/specs/main.html#middleware). I'm going to recommend this middleware tho: [TimingMiddleware](https://github.com/steinnes/timing-asgi).

The FastAPI `@app.add_middleware` is inherited from `Starlette`, so you should be able to do as: https://github.com/steinnes/timing-asgi#usage.

**Note:**
There's an issue on `Starlette` side to [improve the documentation about middlewares](https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/1029). So, I think a PR will be gladly accepted by @tiangolo (those are my words, not his, jfyk) if someone wants to improve the documentation about it on [Advanced Middleware section](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/middleware/) on FastAPI itself.

comments.body: Thanks, ycd! It looks better. While frankly, it still has some performance gap to the level of Go/Java.

comments.author: ycd
comments.body:
Since FastAPI is built with Python i do not think comparing Java frameworks and Python frameworks is a fair comparison, FastAPI says I'm one of the fastest framework available in Python and my performance is on par with NodeJS and Go, so here is a small conclusion from @dbanty,  about NodeJS and Go's performance when compared with FastAPI, 


## FastAPI's Performance
If you ask "is it fast" about anything, there will be evidence both for and against. I think the point of linking to TechEmpower instead of listing numbers directly is so that people can explore on their own and see if FastAPI makes sense for their workloads. However, we may be able to do a better job of guiding people about what is "fast" about FastAPI.

For the numbers I'm about to share, I'm using TechEmpowers "Round 19" looking at only the "micro" classification (which is what FastAPI falls under) for "go", "javascript", "typescript", and "python". I don't use Go or NodeJS in production, so I'm picking some popular frameworks which appear under this micro category to compare: "ExpressJS" (javascript), "NestJS" (typescript), and "Gin" (golang). I don't know how their feature sets compare to FastAPI.

## Plain Text
I believe this is what most of the comparisons above me are using. FastAPI is much slower than nest/express which is much slower than Gin. Exactly what people are saying above. If your primary workload is serving text, go with Go.

## Data Updates
Requests must fetch data from a database, update, and commit it back, then serialize and return the result to the caller. Here FastAPI is much faster than NestJS/Express which are much faster than Gin.

## Fortunes
This test uses an ORM and HTML templating. Here all the frameworks are very close to each other but, in order from fastest to slowest, were Gin, NestJS, FastAPI, Express.

## Multiple Queries
This is just fetching multiple rows from the database and serializing the results. Here, FastAPI slightly edges out Gin. Express and NestJS are much slower in this test.

## Single query
Single row is fetched and serialized. Gin is much faster than the rest which are, in order, FastAPI, NestJS, and Express.

## JSON serialization
No database activity, just serializing some JSON. Gin blows away the competition. Express, then Nest, then FastAPI follow.

So the general theme of all the tests combined seems to be if you're working with large amounts of data from the database, FastAPI is the fastest of the bunch. The less database activity (I/O bound), the further FastAPI falls and Gin rises. The real takeaway here is that the answer to "is it fast" is always "it depends". However, we can probably do a better job of pointing out FastAPI's database strengths in the sections talking about speed.

comments.author: victoraugustolls
comments.body:
Sorry for letting me in the conversation, but in the topics described where FastAPI falls off in terms of performance, those could be resolved using, for example, `orjson` for json serialization, and integrating Cython on the stack (uvicorn, Starlette and FastAPI). One excellent article about this can be read here:

https://www.nexedi.com/NXD-Blog.Multicore.Python.HTTP.Server

Some parts of the stack already are Cython based, like uvloop and httptools.

In terms of database, if using Postgres, asyncpg is also Cython based and is great.

issues.title: Add __all__ to __init__.py files to silence mypy(strict) errors
issues.body:
Strict mypy mode gives such errors:
```
base/api/users/controllers.py:4: error: Module 'fastapi' has no attribute 'Depends'
base/api/users/controllers.py:4: error: Module 'fastapi' has no attribute 'HTTPException'
```

on such import statement:
```
from fastapi import Depends, HTTPException
```

Tried using 

```
from fastapi import Depends as Depends
from fastapi import HTTPException as HTTPException
```
as per recommendations in https://github.com/tiangolo/typer/issues/112 discussion. But the errors remain.

It seems that adding __all__ to the __init__.py files for the stuff that's reexported is a way to go (as per https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/7042 discussion).

Thanks for considering this!

I think it makes sense to use this configuration as FastAPI is based on it.

issues.title: Understanding Depends
issues.body:
I'm trying to understand the need for Depends. While I can see its use, I don't see how it differs from directly calling a function. For instance, in the fullstack project the [get_db() ](https://github.com/tiangolo/full-stack-fastapi-postgresql/blob/master/%7B%7Bcookiecutter.project_slug%7D%7D/backend/app/app/api/deps.py) is consistently used in a Depends. Why not simply call the get_db() within the route?

I apologize if its a dumb question, but I really can't find an appropriate answer.

comments.author: clmnin
comments.body:
The answer can be found at this [stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61871147/what-are-the-advantages-of-using-depends-in-fastapi-over-just-calling-a-dependen) page.

You can also read FastAPI doc on dependencies - [Link](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/)

In essence, FastAPI uses `Depends` for dependency injection - i.e. to declare things that are required for it (a function for example) to work. It is a way to be pedantic of what your function needs.

comments.author: falkben
comments.body:
The other reason, specifically in the case of get_db(), is that you can handle the open/close of the database in the Depends function.  You can be assured that the database conn. will get closed in that case.  

An alternative that is common in Python is using context managers to automatically call a close method.  But if you use that approach, it's not always closed correctly, in my experience.

For instance, if you open some resource (e.g. a db conn or a file) with a context manager and from inside the context manager your return a StreamingResponse, I've run into issues. However, if you use a Depends method, with a try/yield/finally, as shown in the docs, everything gets cleaned up correctly, even using a StreamingResponse.

comments.author: clmnin
comments.body: @falkben oh woah! I didn't know context manager's could fail. That's a great piece of information 👍🏻

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Also, FastAPI extracts parameters from dependencies for OpenAPI and the docs, and they can be overridden for tests.

issues.title: how to get all query params include query parameters that have default value as dict?
issues.body:
```Python
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, Request, Response
from typing import Optional
from jinjasql import JinjaSql


app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/items/")
async def read_item(request: Request, user_id: Optional[int] = 0, project_id: Optional[int] = 0):
    request_query_params = {item[0]: item[1] for item in request.query_params.multi_items() }
    print(request_query_params)

    template = """
    select username, sum(spend)
    from transactions
    where 1 = 1
    {% if user_id %}
    and user_id = {{ user_id }}
    {% endif %}
    {% if project_id %}
    and project_id = {{ project_id }}
    {% endif %}
    """
    j = JinjaSql()
    query, bind_params = j.prepare_query(template, request_query_params)

    return query
```

### Description

* Open the browser and call the endpoint `http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/?project_id=2`.
* It returns `"\n    select username, sum(spend)\n    from transactions\n    where 1 = 1\n    \n    \n    and project_id = %s\n    \n    "`
* query parameter `user_id ` that has a default value 0 is missing.

I wish to have `request_query_params` include the default value too. It can save some typing when there is a lot of query parameters.

### Environment

* OS: Windows
* FastAPI Version 0.61.1:
* Python version: 3.8.2

comments.author: includeamin
comments.body:
Pydantic and Depends solve your problem
``` python
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, Request, Response
from typing import Optional
from jinjasql import JinjaSql
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()


class ParamsModel(BaseModel):
    user_id: Optional[int] = 0
    project_id: Optional[int] = 0


@app.get("/items/", tags=['amin'])
async def read_item(request: Request, params: ParamsModel = Depends()):
    print(params)

    template = """
    select username, sum(spend)
    from transactions
    where 1 = 1
    {% if user_id %}
    and user_id = {{ user_id }}
    {% endif %}
    {% if project_id %}
    and project_id = {{ project_id }}
    {% endif %}
    """
    j = JinjaSql()
    query, bind_params = j.prepare_query(template, params.dict())

    return query

```

comments.author: shizidushu
comments.body:
Just find that `locals` works.
```python
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, Request, Response
from typing import Optional
from jinjasql import JinjaSql

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/items/")
async def read_item(request: Request, user_id: Optional[int] = None, project_id: Optional[int] = 0):
    params = locals()

    template = """
    select username, sum(spend)
    from transactions
    where 1 = 1
    {% if user_id or user_id is not none %}
    and user_id = {{ user_id }}
    {% endif %}
    {% if project_id or project_id is not none %}
    and project_id = {{ project_id }}
    {% endif %}
    """
    j = JinjaSql()
    query, bind_params = j.prepare_query(source = template, data = params)

    return query, bind_params
```

issues.title: Using neomodel's data models in place of pydantic
issues.body:
I am making a `fastapi` server with `neo4j` database, and am using the `neomodel` driver for connecting to the database.
I will have to create 2 seperate models, first in neomodel, second in pydantic (for request body validation and stuff). Is there a way to use the neomodel models in all the places where pydantic models are used in fastapi, if not could anyone point me in a direction to make the development experience as frictionless as possible.

Also, I am open to making a PR (if this is a feature that can be implemented), I'll just need a little guidance because I don't know the fastapi codebase.

BTW, thanks for the amazing library!

comments.author: Kludex
comments.body:
I don't think there's space for that on the FastAPI code source. I see it as analogous of what we do when we use SQLAlchemy: create both Pydantic and SQLAlchemy models. 

What I've checked is that there's a package called [django-neomodel](https://github.com/neo4j-contrib/django-neomodel) (last commit 2 years ago) that could give you ideas on a possible package called `fastapi-neomodel`. Similar to what @tiangolo is doing for SQLAlchemy + Pydantic.

comments.author: dvjn
Kludex
comments.body:
To be honest, `pydantic-sqlalchemy` was not the one I was talking about. I was talking about [this](https://twitter.com/tiangolo/status/1287679028036599809). It's not available yet, and I don't know if it will, but the idea I was referring is that one.

> but then it seems like I will lose so many features in pydantic like constraints, because there is limited support for most of them in neomodel (afaik).

Well, if you want an interface between those two, it seems fair to limit one based on the other.

> Maybe asking for pydantic models support in neomodel is a better approach. Your thoughts?

I don't see why not. :+1:
