issues.title: Questions should be encouraged to be posted on Stackoverflow
issues.body:
Of the **open 345 issues** (at the time of writing) **219 are questions**. 

Since Github doesn't have a feature to moderate questions (upvote, downvote, close a question, etc) it can be hard to regulate quality in the repo. We could use Github Discussion (example usage by [vercel](https://github.com/vercel/vercel/discussions/) and [edgedb](https://github.com/edgedb/edgedb/discussions/)) but for a large community like FastAPI it would be better if questions are redirected to Stack Overflow where questions quality can be regulated.

comments.author: clmnin
comments.body:
@tiangolo do you agree with this? If so we could select a few moderators and start working towards this goal.

The benefits with StackOverflow are:
1. Comes in one's google search (here in github we `close issues` and they get hidden from our default view)
2. Can be linked as an answer to a new question (a duplicate as SO calls it)
3. Creates a database of questions and answers that future devs can count on (staying true to StackOverflow's mission)

comments.author: Kludex
comments.body:
I like the idea about the Github discussions. If this is approved, remember to change the [documentation here](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/help-fastapi/#help-others-with-issues-in-github).

Also, I think is relevant so... You guys should be aware, but I'm going to point out that most of the questions about FastAPI are neither on Github nor StackOverflow. They are on our Gitter, and those are lost over time due to its search engine. So I'm going to reinforce the idea previously suggested on another issue: change to Discord. There are [several open source communities already using Discord](https://discord.com/open-source), [Vue.js](https://vuejs.org/) being one of them. 

> Since Github doesn't have a feature to moderate questions (upvote, downvote, close a question, etc) it can be hard to regulate quality in the repo. 

This is not really a problem. A bot can be written to act as you suggested. But I liked the Github discussion, it makes more sense to me. :smile:

issues.title: fastapi build docker and publish to  k8s and i need gunicorn+fastapi or not ?
issues.body:
Linux environment
Question 1：fastapi build docker and publishto  k8s, so do  i need gunicorn+fastapi or not ?? , just uvicorn+fastapi ?
gunicorn+fastapi  -w 1   pk  uvicorn+fastapi,  Which performance is higher ??

Question 2：Can I release directly to the production environment with  uvicorn+fastapi （no gunicorn and build docker to k8s）??

tks vm!!

comments.author: clmnin
comments.body:
Uvicorn's doc for deployment says ([Source](https://www.uvicorn.org/deployment/))
1. Run `uvcorn --reload` from the terminal for local development
2. Run `gunicorn -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker` for production.
3. Additionally run behind Nginx for self-hosted deployments.
4. Finally, run everything behind a CDN for caching support, and serious DDOS protection.

**gunicorn has a pre-fork worker model**

A pre-fork worker model basically means a master creates forks which handle each request. A fork is a completely separate *nix process ([Source](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/25834333/what-exactly-is-a-pre-fork-web-server-model)). And uvicorn is a ASGI server running [uvloop](https://github.com/MagicStack/uvloop) (every python async needs an event loop and you use uvloop here)

So that means gunicorn in front of uvcorn will help you have workers handle requests before it hits uvcorn which itself is a ASGI server. So for scalability you should be using `gunicorn -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker`. Also please go though the uvcorn deployment doc mentioned above.

comments.author: clmnin
comments.body:
To answer your questions
1. Which performance is higher?
**A.** gunicorn on top of uvicorn will perform better (because you have multiple workers/*nix processes which is scheduled by the OS for performance and concurrency)

2. Can I release directly to the production environment with uvicorn+fastapi
**A.** Yes you can

attention this: gunicorn -w 1

gunicorn+fastapi -w 1（1 worker）   pk    uvicorn+fastapi(no gunicorn manage ), Which performance is higher ??

comments.author: clmnin
comments.body:
I'm sorry I didn't quite catch the question the first time around, now amusing `pk` means `vs` here is my answer:

These technologies (gunicorn and k8) aren't as similar as they initially seem. They address different portions of the application stack and are actually complementary.

### Gunicorn
Web request concurrency is primarily limited by network I/O or "I/O bound". These types of tasks can be scaled using cooperative scheduling provided by threads. If you find request concurrency is limiting your application, increasing gunicorn worker threads may well be the place to start

### Kubernetes
Where Kubernetes comes in handy is by providing out-of-the-box horizontal scalability and fault tolerance.

### Uvicorn
Uvicorn is a lightning-fast "ASGI" server. It runs asynchronous Python web code in a **single process**.

So for `gunicorn + uvicorn + fastapi` with one worker deployment what you get is
1. One async worker (like a Gevent that creates a new greenlet for every new request). 
    * Every time a new request comes they are handled by greenlets spawned by the worker threads. The core idea is when there is IO bound action in your code the greenlet will yield and give a handle to another greenlet to use the CPU. So when many requests are coming to your app, it can handle them concurrently, the IO-bound calls are not going to block the CPU and throughput will improve.
2. `uvloop` as your async loop
3. `fastapi` as your code

So for `uvicorn + fastapi` with one worker deployment (no gunicorn) what you get is
1. `uvloop` as your async loop
2. `fastapi` as your code

Here you don't have `greenlets` that take care of IO bound tasks and give you true concurrency.

**Using k8 is just an added stack and doesn't replace your gunicorn**


Answer to question:
**Q** gunicorn+fastapi -w 1（1 worker） pk uvicorn+fastapi(no gunicorn manage ), Which performance is higher ??
**A** `gunicorn+fastapi -w 1` will be performant (note: you should be using uvcorn as it uses uvloop, which is faster than asyncio's implementation)

comments.author: Kludex
comments.body:
> @Gatsby-Lee Could you maybe clarify what seems wrong in the "accepted answer"? It's all quite confusing for beginners. I, for one, don't really understand what the gunicorn greenlets do for us when using fastapi and what is the interplay between these and the uvloop?. Maybe @Kludex has some light to shed over this?

There is no greenlet involved on gunicorn with uvicorn. You can check the way the workers are spawned [here](https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/blob/ff58e0c6da83d5520916bc4cc109a529258d76e1/gunicorn/arbiter.py#L567). 

Gunicorn only manages the workers, in the sense that it will make sure that if you wish to use 4 workers, you'll always have 4 workers (if they die, they are resurrected) . Uvicorn doesn't do that for you, it only spawns the workers, and in case some worker die, it doesn't care.

Giving the paragraph above, you don't **need** Gunicorn for K8s. You can use it, but it's not really needed. If you're not using it, and you are using multiple uvicorn workers, be sure to have a health condition that makes sense for them (considering my previous paragraph).

uvloop is just an event loop implementation. It's faster than the standard `asyncio`, hence it's recommended for production.

comments.body:
###  manager.py
```python

import asyncio
import platform
import uvicorn

if platform.system() != 'Windows':
    import uvloop

    asyncio.set_event_loop_policy(uvloop.EventLoopPolicy())

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import ORJSONResponse

app = FastAPI(title='fastapi-service', default_response_class=ORJSONResponse, openapi_url=None, docs_url=None,
              redoc_url=None, swagger_ui_oauth2_redirect_url=None)


@app.post('/index')
async def index():
    return {'name': 'a big dict as response'}


if __name__ == '__main__':
    uvicorn.run(app, host='0.0.0.0', port=9000, debug=False)

```

###  uvicorn  VS  gunicorn
```bash
uvicorn manager:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 9000 --workers 4

# VS

gunicorn manager:app  -b 0.0.0.0:9001 -w 4 -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker

# result：gunicorn -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker  has higher performance ！！！！
# result2:  advice Gunicorn for K8s

```

### Another 
- url：https://www.techempower.com/benchmarks/#section=data-r20&hw=ph&test=composite
- higher performance:  starlette  >  sanic  > fastapi
- fastapi = starlette  + pydantic ( but  sanic  > fastapi )
- advice:   starlette + pydantic

issues.title: Why Http headers is being vanished when using ORJSONResponse() in fastAPI?
issues.body:
Http headers can be modified in [fastAPI](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/); however when I tried to use faster serializer (in this instance `ORJSONResponse`) in order to have faster response, all added or modified headers confusingly is being vanished. **I don't have any idea that why it happens and how can I fix it?**
also we got the same result for `UJSONResponse` too.

*thanks for sharing your experiences.* 

### A. This code can't modify headers
```
from fastapi import APIRouter, Response
from fastapi.responses import ORJSONResponse
router = APIRouter()

@router.get("/test")
async def plan_list(response:Response):
    response.headers["test-header"]="test-value"
    return ORJSONResponse(list(range(10000)))
```

### Output header list of code A:
```
date: Thu, 22 Oct 2020 18:42:19 GMT
server: uvicorn
content-length: 48891
content-type: application/json
```


### B. This code modifies headers normally 
```
from fastapi import APIRouter, Response
from fastapi.responses import ORJSONResponse
router = APIRouter()

@router.get("/test")
async def plan_list(response:Response):
    response.headers["test-header"]="test-value"
    return list(range(10000))
```
### Output header list of code B:
```
date: Thu, 22 Oct 2020 18:42:19 GMT
server: uvicorn
content-length: 48891
content-type: application/json
test-header: test-value
```

test platform:
* OS: macOS 10.15.7
* client: insomnia 2020.14.1
* python: 3.8.5

comments.author: includeamin
comments.body:
try this
``` python
from fastapi import APIRouter, Response
from fastapi.responses import ORJSONResponse

router = APIRouter()


@router.get("/test", response_class=ORJSONResponse)
async def plan_list(response: Response):
    response.headers["test-header"] = "test-value"
    return list(range(10000))

```

Specifically when data is very large object >10 MB I have to use it as function call.

comments.author: includeamin
comments.body:
I'm not sure that it's good to do that or not.
if you want to directly use that:
``` python
from fastapi import APIRouter, Response, FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import ORJSONResponse

app = FastAPI()
router = APIRouter()


@router.get("/test")
async def plan_list():
    response = ORJSONResponse(list(range(10000)))
    response.headers.update({"test-header": "test-value"})
    return response


app.include_router(router)

```

app = FastAPI()
router = APIRouter()


@router.get("/test")
async def plan_list(response:Response):
       response.headers["test"]="test-value"
       return ORJSONResponse(list(range(10000)),headers=response.headers)



app.include_router(router)
```

issues.title: is there a way to add more metadata (like uuid) for path operation itself?
issues.body:
I want to assign an unique identifier for each path operation of a router. Then I can collect the `app.openapi()` data and put it into database for further use.

`operationid`, `path`, `summary`, `description` of a path operation can be changed easily and it is not clear to be unique and unchangeable.

I wish there is something like
```python
@router.get(
    "/get_orders",
    uuid = "2dd94d0d-0862-4a04-ad54-ac478ac4b7e9"
)
```
Obviously, the above code doesn't work because there is no `uuid` argument.

Is there is a way to get around?

comments.author: ycd
comments.body:
You can do something like this, which will create you a unique operationID based on your function name.

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute

app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/items/")
async def read_items():
    return [{"item_id": "Foo"}]


def use_route_names_as_operation_ids(app: FastAPI) -> None:
    """
    Simplify operation IDs so that generated API clients have simpler function
    names.

    Should be called only after all routes have been added.
    """
    for route in app.routes:
        if isinstance(route, APIRoute):
            route.operation_id = route.name  # in this case, 'read_items'


use_route_names_as_operation_ids(app)
```

Reference: [Using the path operation function name as the operationId](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration/)

comments.author: shizidushu
comments.body: @ycd Thank you for your reply. Function name can be changed easily too. I like `uuid` if I can specify because it gives others (includes future me) a strong hint that there is no need to change it.

comments.author: shizidushu
comments.body: Just tried to use function name as operationId. Fastapi seems doesn't enforce the `operationId` to be unique.

comments.author: septatrix
comments.body:
Regarding your initial question the automatically assigned `operationId` will always be unique as it combines function name, endpoint path and HTTP method into the id which in combination _should_ never be the same (though FastAPI does not enforce this)

> Just tried to use function name as operationId. Fastapi seems doesn't enforce the `operationId` to be unique.

Personally I would like if FastAPI would enforce unique operationIds (and unique method-path-name tuples) when manually assigning them however as you observed it currently does not.

I do not think we can implement this to FastAPI, Because our current encoder implementation does this.

```python
isinstance(obj, class)

If we are going to do that to `numpy.array`, which will look something like this.

```python
if isinstance(obj, np.array)
```

comments.author: eseglem
comments.body:
This error is not part of the FastAPI codebase. It appears to come from [uvicorn](https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/blob/master/uvicorn/protocols/http/h11_impl.py#L263) and `fastapi.WebSocket` is a wrapper around [`starlette.websockets.WebSocket`](https://www.starlette.io/websockets/)

How are you calling `uvicorn` for your app? 

Perhaps: [uvicorn/test_websocket.py](https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/blob/master/tests/protocols/test_websocket.py) or [starlette/test_websocket.py](https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/master/tests/test_websockets.py) might be a good place to look to see how they are configuring the tests.

As suspected, this was a uvicorn issue, not FastAPI, however, to fix this, for anyone else curious, goto terminal and type

```bash
python3 -m pip uninstall uvicorn
```

then

```bash
python3 -m pip install uvicorn[standard]
```

The original uvicorn package doesnt come with websocket support, you have to download the standard one.

issues.title: Subclass fastapi.params.Security to extend JWT payload checks for route authorization(?)
issues.body:
### First check

* [x] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
* [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
* [x] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
* [x] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
* [x] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).
* [x] After submitting this, I commit to one of:
    * Read open issues with questions until I find 2 issues where I can help someone and add a comment to help there.
    * I already hit the "watch" button in this repository to receive notifications and I commit to help at least 2 people that ask questions in the future.
    * Implement a Pull Request for a confirmed bug.

### Example

Here's a self-contained, minimal, reproducible, example with my use case:

```Python
import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum, unique
from typing import (
    Any,
    Callable,
    List,
    Mapping,
    Optional,
    Sequence,
    Type,
)

import requests
from fastapi import (
    APIRouter,
    Depends,
    FastAPI,
    HTTPException,
    status,
)
from fastapi.params import Security
from fastapi.security.oauth2 import OAuth2AuthorizationCodeBearer
from jose import jwt
from jose.constants import ALGORITHMS
from pydantic import BaseModel, BaseSettings

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class Config(BaseSettings):
    AUTH0_DOMAIN: str
    AUTH0_AUDIENCE: str

    class Config:
        env_file = ".env"
        case_sensitive = True
        validate_all = True
        allow_mutation = False


config = Config()


@dataclass
class JwtPayload:
    iss: str
    sub: str
    aud: List[str]
    iat: int
    exp: int
    azp: str
    scope: str
    permissions: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)


@unique
class Permission(Enum):
    """Enum values match permissions configured in Auth0"""
    THING_READ = "read:thing"
    THING_WRITE = "write:thing"


@dataclass
class Auth0User:
    token: str
    payload: JwtPayload
    permissions: List[Permission]

    @property
    def id(self) -> str:
        return self.payload.sub

    def has_permissions(self, permissions: List[Permission]) -> bool:
        return bool(permissions) and all(p in self.permissions for p in permissions)


def get_permissions(payload: JwtPayload, all_permissions: Type[Permission]) -> List[Permission]:
    """Parse permission strings into corresponding Enums"""
    permissions = []
    available_permissions_map = {r.value: r for r in all_permissions}
    for p_value in payload.permissions:
        if p := available_permissions_map.get(p_value):
            permissions.append(p)
        else:
            logger.warning(f"unrecognized: {p_value}")
    return permissions


def get_jwk(domain: str, token: str) -> Optional[Mapping[str, Any]]:
    """Get pubkey associated with Auth0 account that matches token's KID"""
    header = jwt.get_unverified_header(token)
    jwks = requests.get(f"https://{domain}/.well-known/jwks.json").json()
    return next((key for key in jwks["keys"] if key.get("kid") == header["kid"]), None)


oauth2_scheme = OAuth2AuthorizationCodeBearer(
    authorizationUrl=config.AUTH0_DOMAIN, tokenUrl=config.AUTH0_AUDIENCE,
)


# Question: does the value for `permissions` come from the route dependency? ex: Auth0Security(current_user, permissions=[Permission.THING_READ])
async def current_user(
    permissions: Optional[Sequence[Permission]], token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)
) -> Auth0User:
    rsa_key = get_jwk(config.AUTH0_DOMAIN, token)
    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(
            token,
            rsa_key,
            algorithms=[ALGORITHMS.RS256],
            audience=config.AUTH0_AUDIENCE,
            issuer=f"https://{config.AUTH0_DOMAIN}",
        )
    except Exception:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED, detail="unauthorized")

    jwt_payload = JwtPayload(**payload)
    user = Auth0User(
        token=token, payload=jwt_payload, permissions=get_permissions(jwt_payload, Permission)
    )

    if permissions and not user.has_permissions(permissions):
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED, detail="insufficient permissions",
        )

    return user


class Auth0SecurityParam(Security):
    """Following pattern like fastapi.params.Security"""
    def __init__(
        self,
        dependency: Optional[Callable[..., Any]] = None,
        *,
        scopes: Optional[Sequence[str]] = None,
        permissions: Optional[Sequence[Permission]] = None,
        use_cache: bool = True,
    ):
        super().__init__(dependency=dependency, scopes=scopes, use_cache=use_cache)
        self.permissions = list(permissions or [])


def Auth0Security(
    dependency: Optional[Callable[..., Any]] = None,
    *,
    scopes: Optional[Sequence[str]] = None,
    permissions: Optional[Sequence[Permission]] = None,
    use_cache: bool = True,
) -> Any:
    """Following pattern like fastapi.param_functions.Security"""
    return Auth0SecurityParam(
        dependency=dependency, scopes=scopes, permissions=permissions, use_cache=use_cache
    )


hello = APIRouter()


class OutModel(BaseModel):
    hello: str


@hello.get("/", response_model=OutModel)
async def hello_world(
    *, user: Auth0User = Auth0Security(current_user, permissions=[Permission.THING_READ]),
):
    return {"hello": "world"}


router = APIRouter()
router.include_router(hello, prefix="/hello", tags=["hello"])

app = FastAPI()
app.include_router(router, prefix="/v1")
```

### Description

[Auth0's core RBAC can include `permissions` in the JWT payload](https://auth0.com/docs/authorization/rbac/enable-role-based-access-control-for-apis) along with `scopes`. Given the documented ability to use [`Security(factory, scopes=["me"])`](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/security/oauth2-scopes/#security-in-decorator-dependencies) as deps for a route, I thought it may be possible to subclass `Security` to extend this functionality to verify the `permissions` field of a JWT's payload. This doesn't appear to work as expected.

Should this be possible? Or is there a different way that FastAPI recommends achieving this?

As commented in the minimal example: does the value for `permissions` come from the route dependency? ex: `Auth0Security(current_user, permissions=[Permission.THING_READ])`

### Steps to reproduce

* Open the browser and call the endpoint `/v1/hello` with a valid `Authorization: Bearer <jwt>` header that includes `"permissions":["read:thing"]` in the token's payload
* It returns 422: `{"detail":[{"loc":["body"],"msg":"field required","type":"value_error.missing"}]}`
* But I expected it to return `{"hello": "world"}`.

### Environment

* OS: Linux
* FastAPI Version: 0.61.1
* Python version: 3.8.5

### Additional context

* I have been using Flask in production for years. I am trying-out FastAPI for the first time.

comments.author: cahna
Mause
comments.body: Generally you would just use the scopes for that purpose - is there a reason why you can't?

comments.author: cahna
comments.body:
Thanks for your response. Existing employer infrastructure is the reason. In this case, `scopes` are used to define which services the bearer of the token may access, and `permissions` are used to define fine resource-level controls.

Given [how flexible JWT claims can be](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-oauth-json-web-token-32#section-4.2), I am hoping FastAPI does not restrict JWT-based authz to a single implementation spec. There are several similar use cases I may need to support.

For another example, say there is a collision-resistant claim named `https://my.example.com/api/foo`. I would want to be able to extend the `Security` dependency to only allow values of `payload["https://my.example.com/api/foo"]` that match a given list.

comments.author: eseglem
cahna
comments.body:
I based my initial implementation on the [Oauth2 Scopes example](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/security/oauth2-scopes/#oauth2-scopes) from the Advanced User Guide (referenced [at the bottom of the JWT example](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt/#advanced-usage-with-scopes)). The [documentation](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/security/oauth2-scopes/#security-in-decorator-dependencies) and [code](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/dca9cc3ec516b048fadfa42778a7763ad277c7ae/fastapi/params.py#L329) for `fastapi.params.Security` led me to believe that `Security(...)` can be used in the same way as `Depends(...)` (in fact, `Security` [is a subclass of `Depends`](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/dca9cc3ec516b048fadfa42778a7763ad277c7ae/fastapi/params.py#L316)). For reference, here is the example:

```python
@app.get("/users/me/items/")
async def read_own_items(
    current_user: User = Security(get_current_active_user, scopes=["items"])
):
    return [{"item_id": "Foo", "owner": current_user.username}]
```

It was my hope that I can subclass `Security` to add my own JWT payload checks. Upon closer inspection, however, it seems like `Security` may be a single-purpose interface that cannot be extended. The fact that [`fastapi.dependencies.utils.get_sub_dependent()` has conditional logic for `isinstance(depends, params.Security)`](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/dca9cc3ec516b048fadfa42778a7763ad277c7ae/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L143) leads me to believe that this may be the case. 

If `Security` is _only_ meant to be used for authz based _only_ on `scopes`, then I will definitely use your recommendation. It would be great, however, if [the documented ability to nest `Security` dependencies for a dependency tree](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/security/oauth2-scopes/#more-details-about-securityscopes) could be extended to other parts of a token's payload. Is this possible without a change to framework code, @tiangolo?

comments.author: luisgcss
comments.body:
I have a similar problem. I want to add scopes from Security to openapi, but only OAuth2 and OpenIdConnect are possible to do this. Because here on `fastapi.dependencies.utils.get_sub_dependant` he ignores the others instances. Here the piece of the code:
```python
use_scopes: List[str] = []
if isinstance(dependency, (OAuth2, OpenIdConnect)):
      use_scopes = security_scopes
```

I needed to be:
```python
use_scopes: List[str] = []
if isinstance(dependency, (OAuth2, OpenIdConnect, HTTPBearer)):
      use_scopes = security_scopes
```

There is a way to do that?

issues.title: Function currying for Depends over class instances with __call__()
issues.body:
### Description

Function currying is a rather common technique in functional programming. It can also be called local functions, or closures.
A parent function takes an arbitrary number of arguments, and defines a function inside it using those arguments inside its processing. The generated function (closure), can then ask for different arguments to be executed.
In Python, it relies on the parent function having its own scope, and the nested function being able to access it.

This can be used to generate very flexible callable functions, to use as part of Depends().
To create flexible parametrized Depends callable, the documentation only provides examples of a full-blown class instances with requiring both `__init__` and  `__call__` implementations. While most people are probably used to init, `__call__(self)` for a class  is more likely to be a new concept.
I feel like this distracts from more minimal implementations, that don't need OOP everything, especially when this is literally begging for functional style. 
Such complex class + class instance implementation might be granted when it acts on a state it holds and need to mutate as part of its logic, but most cases should be fine with currying.

### Example

```
# generic currying function definition
def currying_filter(key: str, value: str):
    def filter(data: Dict[str: List[str]]):
        data_values = data.get(key, None)
        for item in data_values:
            if item == value:
                return item
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="No needle found!")
    return filter

# curried function
needle_in_data = currying_filter(key='hay', value='needle')
other_filter = currying_filter(key='book_name', value='Combinatory Logic.')


# which can then be used as such
needle_in_data(data)
```
For a more real-life use case, the nested filter can declare a `Depends()` too, and be depended itself, so endpoints can easily implement various fine-grained checks without needing to implement a more complex class with `__init__` and `__call__` 

```
# like above example but calls a depends. with anything that will return some data.
    def filter(data: dict = Depends(data_source):
        # etc

# then a curried filter can be called in endpoints
@router.post('hay')
async def work_with_needles(needle: str = Depends(needle_in_data):
    # process as usual, with the guaratee that the needle has been filtered upstream.

```
### Other thoughts
While of course, many people are not familiar with functional programming style, or even assume it is possible in Python,
such techniques are already widely spread, and only uses already known common syntax for functions, with the only catch being that it is nested.
It can be introduced as another technique, likely simpler to implement, to generate callables to use as a Depends() callable.

One advantage of using classes instead of curried function is that, being class instances, they have a type associated with it, so one might think that it benefits type hinting. However, the return value of such callables does not have to be the type of the callable itself, so the expectation of typing can actually introduce more confusion. Furthermore, there is no typing inside the call for Depends itself, only for the returned value.

I would like to put some documentation page explaining the technique, or at least some explanation why the technique isn't used, and instead documentation (and internal source code, from I see) makes extensive use of the `__call__()` style, even with no inner data.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Hey there! Yep, that works too. I think you could write a blog post about it.

I wouldn't want to add it to the docs and add extra complexity to the concepts explained as that's a technique to generate a function, and not something different from what is already supported. But it's probably worth an independent blog post explaining it.

issues.title: ValueError in BaseModel for query parameters causes Internal Server Error
issues.body:
Hi, we are using models to validate and parse query parameters. 
Rising `ValueError` in custom validator causes `Internal Server Error`, while it should be 422 with error description. 

```Python

from pydantic import BaseModel, validator
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI

app = FastAPI()


class QP(BaseModel):
    param_a: int = 2

    @validator('param_a')
    def validate_a(cls, v, values):
        raise ValueError('Bad parameter')


@app.get("/test_bad")
async def test_request(qp: QP = Depends()):
    # this one fails with Internal Server Error
    return 'Ok'


@app.post("/test_ok")
async def test_request(qp: QP):
    # this one works fine, responding with 422 and error description
    return 'Ok'
```

`GET` request to `/test_bad` or `/test_bad?param_a=1` results in 500, calling `/test_bad?param_a=a` results in 422 which is ok (it fails before custom validator due to bad type).
Using same model, but for body works as expected. (`POST` to `/test_ok` with body `{"param_a":1}` result in 422)

### Environment

* OS: macOS 10.15.6 (19G73), Darwin Kernel Version 19.6.0
* FastAPI Version: 0.61.1
* Pydantic Version: 1.6.1
* Python version: 3.6.8 and 3.7.4

### Additional context

Stacktrace:
```
INFO:     127.0.0.1:53141 - "GET /test_bad HTTP/1.1" 500 Internal Server Error
ERROR:    Exception in ASGI application
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/uvicorn/protocols/http/h11_impl.py", line 389, in run_asgi
    result = await app(self.scope, self.receive, self.send)
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/uvicorn/middleware/proxy_headers.py", line 45, in __call__
    return await self.app(scope, receive, send)
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/fastapi/applications.py", line 179, in __call__
    await super().__call__(scope, receive, send)
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/starlette/applications.py", line 111, in __call__
    await self.middleware_stack(scope, receive, send)
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/starlette/middleware/errors.py", line 181, in __call__
    raise exc from None
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/starlette/middleware/errors.py", line 159, in __call__
    await self.app(scope, receive, _send)
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/starlette/exceptions.py", line 82, in __call__
    raise exc from None
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/starlette/exceptions.py", line 71, in __call__
    await self.app(scope, receive, sender)
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/starlette/routing.py", line 566, in __call__
    await route.handle(scope, receive, send)
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/starlette/routing.py", line 227, in handle
    await self.app(scope, receive, send)
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/starlette/routing.py", line 41, in app
    response = await func(request)
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/fastapi/routing.py", line 176, in app
    dependency_overrides_provider=dependency_overrides_provider,
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py", line 552, in solve_dependencies
    solved = await run_in_threadpool(call, **sub_values)
  File "/project/dir/env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/starlette/concurrency.py", line 34, in run_in_threadpool
    return await loop.run_in_executor(None, func, *args)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/3.7.4_1/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.7/lib/python3.7/concurrent/futures/thread.py", line 57, in run
    result = self.fn(*self.args, **self.kwargs)
  File "pydantic/main.py", line 346, in pydantic.main.BaseModel.__init__
pydantic.error_wrappers.ValidationError: 1 validation error for QP
param_a
  Bad parameter (type=value_error)
```

comments.author: LindezaGrey
comments.body:
Hey, 
Instead of ValueError try using HTTPException from fastapi


```python
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel, validator

app = FastAPI()


class QP(BaseModel):
    param_a: int = 2

    @validator("param_a")
    def validate_a(cls, v, values):
        raise HTTPException(422, "Bad parameter")


@app.get("/test_bad")
async def test_request(qp: QP = Depends()):
    # this one fails with Internal Server Error
    return "Ok"


@app.post("/test_ok")

    # this one works fine, responding with 422 and error description

comments.author: Dock1100
comments.body:
> ```python
>  raise HTTPException(422, "Bad parameter")
> ```

Yeah, this kind of works. But what if we have more parameters? What if I need to reuse another model with works well for body validation? How API client can find out, which parameter is bad? ...

comments.author: Dock1100
comments.body:
Well, after a bit of debugging, looks like the error is within `fastapi > dependencies > utils.py : request_params_to_args`, it should work similar  to `fastapi > dependencies > utils.py : request_body_to_args`.  The`request_params_to_args` skips some `@validator`, after which parent call `fastapi > dependencies > utils.py : solve_dependencies : 553` (`solved = await run_in_threadpool(call, **sub_values)`) tries to rebuild model with not fully verified data, and fails.

Tried to make some quick fix, but no luck.

comments.author: eseglem
comments.body:
Using `ValueError` should automatically trigger a `HTTPException` in FastAPI. I wouldn't change that part. 

`Depends` is not for resolving Pydantic models, its for resolving functions. I would not expect them to behave the same. Using a Pydantic model to parse query params doesn't appear to be supported, its for parsing JSON bodies. 

You could probably do something like this: 
```python 
def parse_params(request: Request):
    return QP(request.query_params._dict)

@app.get("/test_bad")
async def test_request(qp: QP = Depends(parse_params)):
    # this one fails with Internal Server Error
    return "Ok"
```
Probably now how its meant to be used, but could work.

comments.author: Dock1100
comments.body:
@eseglem reasonable. I misunderstood the docs https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/classes-as-dependencies/#use-it, thought it was `pydantic.BaseModel`, not `dataclass`.
Thanks for the clarification.

comments.author: johnraz
comments.body:
For what is worth, there is a workaround here: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1474#issuecomment-803021344

Also, using Pydantic models should be considered valid to describe query parameters as the output of the validation is the same as eg: an Enum used to type a query param.

It would allow to keep consistency when doing more advanced validation for query parameters.

issues.title: RuntimeError for Depends on Python 3.8 stating "...yield require Python 3.7 or above" when testing Router in pytest
issues.body:
### First check

* [x] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
* [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
* [x] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
* [x] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
* [x] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).
* [x] After submitting this, I commit to one of:
    * Read open issues with questions until I find 2 issues where I can help someone and add a comment to help there.
    * I already hit the "watch" button in this repository to receive notifications and I commit to help at least 2 people that ask questions in the future.
    * Implement a Pull Request for a confirmed bug.

### Example

Here's a self-contained, [minimal, reproducible, example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/minimal-reproducible-example) with my use case:

```Python
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

def get_db():
    db = 1
    yield db

router = APIRouter()
client = TestClient(router)

@router.get("/")
def foo(ds=Depends(get_db)):
    print(ds)
    return {}

def test_foo():
    client.get("/")
    assert 1 == 1
```

### Description

The above sample fails with:

> RuntimeError: 
> FastAPI dependencies with yield require Python 3.7 or above,
> or the backports for Python 3.6, installed with:
> pip install async-exit-stack async-generator

However, replacing `router = FastAPI()` with `router = FastAPI()` succeeds. 

I ran into this problem while trying to do some isolated unit testing on the API routes, and I assumed it would have been okay based on the documentation stating:

> You can think of APIRouter as a "mini FastAPI" class.
> All the same options are supported.
> All the same parameters, responses, dependencies, tags, etc.

The workaround is simple, and I can first create an app class in my testing, but I'm more curious about why this doesn't work. Unless I'm doing something incorrectly, maybe this is worth a documentation note, or a clearer exception message?

### Environment

* OS: macOS 10.15.7
* FastAPI Version: 0.61.1
* Python version: 3.8.6

Thanks for the help here everyone! Yeah, that would be a bug in the developers' code, the `TestClient` is supposed to get a FastAPI app, not a router. As the documentation only shows using the `FastAPI` instance with the `TestClient` I wouldn't expect users to use a router with it, so I would prefer not to increase the docs and the stuff people have to learn adding a not about that.

issues.title: Adding custom authentification to swagger UI
issues.body:
We manage authentification using an `Authorization` header bearer. 

The approach so far is:
```
@router.get("/private_route", response_model=List[schemas.Widget])
def get_widgets(db: Session = Depends(get_db), user_session=Depends(get_current_session_payload)):
    return  'hello

def get_current_session_payload(authorization: Optional[str] = Header(None)):
    # some logic here
    return authorization
```

This works fine but fails using the swagger UI because the `authorization` header gets overridden.

Would there be a way to achieve a custom authentication mechanism for swagger UI that would allow setting a custom `Authorization` header?

Thanks a lot

comments.author: HarrySky
comments.body:
Here is solution I use (simplified, `get_user` method and `User` model are not included from codebase):

```python
from typing import Sequence, Any

from fastapi import Request, params
from fastapi.openapi.docs import get_swagger_ui_html
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse, JSONResponse


def Auth(scopes: Sequence[str] = None) -> Any:  # noqa: N802
    return params.Security(dependency=get_user, scopes=scopes, use_cache=False)


async def openapi(
    request: Request, user: User = Auth(scopes=["admin"])  # noqa: B008
) -> JSONResponse:
    """Return OpenAPI JSON."""
    if len(request.app.servers) == 0:
        root_path = request.scope.get("root_path", "").rstrip("/")
        request.app.servers.append({"url": root_path})

    return JSONResponse(request.app.openapi())


async def api_help(
    request: Request, user: User = Auth(scopes=["admin"])  # noqa: B008
) -> HTMLResponse:
    """Return Swagger UI API schema page."""
    root_path = request.scope.get("root_path", "").rstrip("/")
    openapi_url = f"{root_path}/openapi.json"
    return get_swagger_ui_html(
        openapi_url=openapi_url, title=f"{request.app.title} - Swagger UI"
    )


app = FastAPI(
    debug=True,
    title="API",
    openapi_url=None,
    docs_url=None,
    redoc_url=None,
)
app.add_api_route(
    "/openapi.json", openapi, include_in_schema=False, methods=["GET"]
)
app.add_api_route("/help", api_help, include_in_schema=False, methods=["GET"])
```

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
I imagine you want Swagger UI to show the "Authorize" button, right? In that case you would need to use one of the security schemes supported by OpenAPI. For example, a bearer token: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/security/first-steps/#fastapis-oauth2passwordbearer

issues.title: Usage of "sub" in the JWT Token documentation https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt/
issues.body:
From what I've gathered the "sub" or "subject" field in the JWT token(https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1) payload should be locally or globally unique. The documentation uses the username from the form data in the payload. 

Is this a good practice? In a few examples I've found others using a unique id instead like `19898981`.

comments.author: cmflynn
comments.body:
> That code is purely for demo purposes, not intended to be used in a production system

Yep definitely understand. My question is more around how it should work in a production system? AFAIK the spec just says sub should be locally unique in the current context or globally unique. I suppose a username is unique but the username ID in some database is the most unique way to represent that. just curious what the thoughts are of the fastapi devs :)

comments.author: Mause

issues.title: Performace/threading issue - gunicorn workers spend most time waiting for an internal queue
issues.body:
# Gunicorn with Uvicorn workers mostly waits for thread pool queue

**TL;DR -** guvicorn+uvicorn spawn threads to handle requests, but they mostly wait for [this queue](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.8/Lib/concurrent/futures/thread.py#L78) instead of working

### Description

I'm using the same scripts as in [tiangolo's example](https://github.com/tiangolo/uvicorn-gunicorn-docker) to run my app, just a bit streamlined for my workflow. All of my endpoints are sync because I'm using sqlalchemy extensively.

On my small "qa" VPS (2x2 GHz CPU), that I also use to run PostgreSQL, I manage to reach only about 300-350 rps (with locust), so I profiled the app and found out that it spends most of its time waiting for [this queue in thread pool executor](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.8/Lib/concurrent/futures/thread.py#L78).
I tried web concurrency 2 and 4, but it did not matter much. I tried just adding `async` to function definitions, and while it boosted performance about 10%, the behavior was the same.

Am I missing some additional configuration, or is this normal?

### Environment

* OS: Host Ubuntu 18.04
* FastAPI Version: 0.60.2
* Python: 3.8.6
* Gunicorn: 20.0.4
* Uvicorn: 0.10.9

App runs in Docker, command: `gunicorn -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker -c "$GUNICORN_CONF" "$APP_MODULE"`

### py-spy graph
Flame graph does not really change depending on which thread/process is profiled.
![profile3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/30983257/95302812-1e7e4480-088b-11eb-83f7-d64e6484541e.png)
Source SVG: 
[profile3.svg.zip](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/files/5339026/profile3.svg.zip)

comments.author: artslob
comments.body:
Can it be because your app is highly asynchronous so it mostly waits for scheduled tasks? Or app cant handle load more then 350 requests/sec?

>All of my endpoints are sync because I'm using sqlalchemy extensively

As I know sync endpoint are executed in thread pool executor and awaited using asyncio. Maybe you reach limit of available threads? Did you try to use asynchronous database (as https://github.com/encode/databases or https://python-gino.org/ for example)?

comments.author: VoidDruid
artslob
comments.body:
The [function](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/687af44df8caa69312b65d1bd7bf1f05dd1e5778/Lib/concurrent/futures/thread.py#L66) `_worker` you provided is [used](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/687af44df8caa69312b65d1bd7bf1f05dd1e5778/Lib/concurrent/futures/thread.py#L207) later in `ThreadPoolExecutor` as main function of thread-worker. So I assume that this code
```python
        while True:
            work_item = work_queue.get(block=True)
```
means that thread worker waits for work to do, but there is no work. So most of time your applications just waits for work (I assume). Maybe locust cant provide high load for your application, and 350 req/sec is limit of locust?

Also, does graph shows only main gunicorn process or it shows all processes/threads?

---
>During load testing CPU usage is 100%

Do you run tests and application on same machine or on different?

PS: Im not ASGI expert, just trying to help :)

comments.author: VoidDruid
comments.body:
> So most of time your applications just waits for work

That was my conclusion too, after looking at the code, but I do not understand why it does that

> Maybe locust cant provide high load for your application, and 350 req/sec is limit of locust?

That was my first assumption, but 100% cpu usage indicates it can.

> Do you run tests and application on same machine or on different?

Different machines. I did not think about it before, but maybe it actually waits for the network or connection? 🤔 

I recently tried to load test my app again, using other "lighter" endpoints and was able to reach ~450 rps. I also established a baseline on this VPS - "ping" endpoint reaches about 700 rps and a small Django app that I load-tested to eliminate the factor of FastAPI and asgi reached about 500 rps - it is all in the same neighbourhood, roughly speaking.

So it looks like either the gunicorn itself, raw cpu power or locust's measurments that is the main problem - most likely the CPU, but I'm still a bit disappointed by the performance. I will also look into the possible network problems. Closing this issue, as it does not seem to be the problem with FastAPI.

@artslob, thanks for some ideas 🙌

Thanks for the help @artslob ! 🍰 

And thanks for coming back to close it @VoidDruid ☕

issues.title: Depends not works for several layers
issues.body:
Good day! Please tell me how you can solve the following task in Python + FastAPI.

There is a test project:
```
app / main.py - main file
app / routes / users.py -set of api methods
app / repos / factory.py - repository factory
app / repos / user_repository.py - repositories
app / handlers / factory.py - handler factory
app / handlers / users.py - handlers
app / domain / user.py - data class
```

The main and routes structure is the same as in the example https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/bigger-applications/

**In the routes/users.py file:**
```
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends
from ..handlers import factory

router = APIRouter()

@router.get("/users/", tags=["users"])
def read_users(handler=Depends(factory.get_handler)):
    return handler.get_all()
```
**In the handlers/factory.py:**
```
from fastapi import Depends
from .users import UserHandler1

def get_handler(handler=Depends(UserHandler1)):
    return handler
```
**In the handlers/users.py:**
```
from fastapi import Depends
from ..repos import factory

class UserHandler1:
    def __init__(self):
        pass

    def get_all(self, repo=Depends(factory.get_repo)):
        return repo.get_all()
```
**repos/factory.py:**
```
from fastapi import Depends
from ..repos.user_repository import UserRepository

def get_repo(repo=Depends(UserRepository)):
    return repo
```
**repos/user_repository.py:**
```
from ..domain.user import User

class UserRepository:
    def __init__(self):
        pass

    def get_all(self):
        return [User(1, 'A'), User(2, 'B'), User(3, 'C')]
```
**domain/user.py:**
```
class User:
    id: int
    name: str

    def __init__(self, id, name):
        self.id = id
        self.name = name
```

Run hypercorn: app.main:app --reload
Call api method: http://127.0.0.1:8000/users/
**And get the error AttributeError: 'Depends' object has no attribute 'get_all'**

If you remove the level of handlers and do this, then everything will work.
**routes/users.py:**
```
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends
from ..repos import factory

router = APIRouter()

@router.get("/users/", tags=["users"])
def read_users(repo=Depends(factory.get_repo)):
    return repo.get_all()
```

It also works if you completely remove all Depends and create
UserRepository and UserHandler1 directly in factories.

**QUESTION: How do I use Depends in this case and why doesn't it work?**

I attach an archive with a test project
[test.zip](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/files/5325883/test.zip)

comments.author: Kludex
comments.body: `UserHandler1` is not a callable. You need to implement `__call__` there. `Depends` expects a callable.

comments.author: Mause
Kludex
comments.body: You're right. What I said doesn't make any sense. 🧐👍

comments.author: dmpyatin
comments.body: @Mause, Many thanks! Your example helped a lot

comments.author: Mause
comments.body: Feel free to close the issue if that solves it :)

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the help here @Mause and @Kludex! 🙇 

And thanks for coming back to close the issue @dmpyatin ☕ 

> Sorry for the long delay! 🙈  I wanted to personally address each issue/PR and they piled up through time, but now I'm checking each one in order.

issues.title: The field ctx is not a part of ValidationError schema
issues.body:
ValidationErrors actually include a ctx parameter, this is great and useful but it's unfortunately not documented by the OpenAPI-schema which causes problems with tools relying on schema correctness as well as confusion for API consumers. I'd categorise this as bug considering it's most likely unintentional undocumented behaviour.

### First check

* [x] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
* [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
* [x] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
* [x] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
* [x] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).
* [x] After submitting this, I commit to one of:
    * Read open issues with questions until I find 2 issues where I can help someone and add a comment to help there.
    * I already hit the "watch" button in this repository to receive notifications and I commit to help at least 2 people that ask questions in the future.
    * Implement a Pull Request for a confirmed bug.

<!-- 

I'm asking all this because answering questions and solving problems in GitHub issues consumes a lot of time. I end up not being able to add new features, fix bugs, review Pull Requests, etc. as fast as I wish because I have to spend too much time handling issues.

All that, on top of all the incredible help provided by a bunch of community members that give a lot of their time to come here and help others.

That's a lot of work they are doing, but if more FastAPI users came to help others like them just a little bit more, it would be much less effort for them (and you and me 😅).

-->

### Example

Here's a self-contained, [minimal, reproducible, example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/minimal-reproducible-example) with my use case:

<!-- Replace the code below with your own self-contained, minimal, reproducible, example, if I (or someone) can copy it, run it, and see it right away, there's a much higher chance I (or someone) will be able to help you -->

```Python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import Body

app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/")
def post_undocumented(
    data_param: str = Body(..., min_length=16)
):
    # Returned value is irrelevant for example
    return {"Hello": "World"}
```

### Description

<!-- Replace the content below with your own problem, question, or error -->

* Open the browser and call post to the endpoint `/`, using `{ "data_param": "a" }`.
* It returns a JSON with validation error containing the field `ctx`.
* But I expected it to return a object matching openapi.json's represenation of validation error.
```json
{
  "title": "ValidationError",
  "required": [
    "loc",
    "msg",
    "type"
  ],
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "loc": {
      "title": "Location",
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "type": "string"
      }
    },
    "msg": {
      "title": "Message",
      "type": "string"
    },
    "type": {
      "title": "Error Type",
      "type": "string"
    }
  }
}
```

### Environment

* OS: [e.g. Linux / Windows / macOS]: N/A, but Linux
* FastAPI Version [e.g. 0.3.0]: 0.54.2
* Python version: 3.8


### Additional context

<!-- Add any other context or screenshots about the question here. -->
Don't think any additional information is needed

comments.body: Looks like it needs to be added here: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/openapi/utils.py#L33

comments.body:

comments.author: Mause
comments.body:
Your issue is here: https://github.com/maurosbicego/rowmate/blob/master/api/models/boat.py#L14

```python
class Boat(BaseModel):
    id: Binary = Binary(bytes(bytearray(uuid4().bytes)), UUID_SUBTYPE)
````

remove the default, and that should fix it (because pydantic tries to encode the binary uuid as a string for the openapi.json)

```

comments.author: Mause
comments.body:
Please provide a full reproduction of the issue, as there appear to be no issues with the code in your issue

```python
import os
import time
from urllib.parse import quote_plus, urlparse

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse
from fastapi.templating import Jinja2Templates
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

app = FastAPI()

templates = Jinja2Templates('.')


def load_homepage(data):
    return 1, 'http://whatever', 'http://whatever'

# your code starts here

@app.get("/", response_class=HTMLResponse)
def homepage(data: str, request: Request):
    ws_url = urlparse(request.url.query)
    ws_url = (
        'http://'
        + request.url.hostname
        + ':'
        + os.environ.get('SOCKET_PORT', default='5001')
        + '/mask'
    )
    user_id, callback_url, source_url = load_homepage(data)
    if source_url != '':
        source_url = 'http://dev-editor.com/download?sourceUrl=' + quote_plus(
            source_url
        )
    return templates.TemplateResponse(
        'homepage.html',
        {
            "request": request,
            'ws_url': ws_url,
            'user_id': user_id,
            'callback_url': callback_url,
            'source_url': source_url,
            'time': str(time.time()),
        },
    )

# your code ends here

tc = TestClient(app)
r = tc.get('/', params={'data': 'whatever'})
assert r.status_code == 200, r.text

comments.author: ycd
atellier
comments.body:
I have the same issue where I want `Enums` to return the `name` and not the `value` of the object. I ended up creating my own `JSONResponse` and had to wrap all my return in to a `JSONResponse(...)`. It would have been nice to just use the `response_class` option, but because `serialize_response` is called before my custom `JSONResponse`, it does not work.

Looks like on the `pydantic` side there is also no real easy way to use our own JSON serializer - which would be very useful since people may want to do serialization differently (for instance when returning `nan` from pandas / numpy).

Any chance of that making to a PR or it that too much of a change that it would break things?

Thanks

comments.author: nguyent
comments.body:
> Looks like on the `pydantic` side there is also no real easy way to use our own JSON serializer - which would be very useful since people may want to do serialization differently (for instance when returning `nan` from pandas / numpy).

+1

I'm currently using FastAPI for an API migration and want to use a custom json encoder for parity reasons. 
I discovered this after diffing responses from both APIs. 

A small example of what I'm seeing (this timestamp is from a pandas dataframe): 

```
legacy timestamp: "2022-01-06 00:00:00+00:00"
fastapi timestamp: "2022-01-06T00:00:00+00:00"
```


### Current FastAPI code

(shortened version of the [current code](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/routing.py#L226-L250) in `get_request_handler`)
```python
raw_response = await run_endpoint_function(...)
response_data = await serialize_response(response_content=raw_response, ...)
response = actual_response_class(response_data, **response_args)
```

`serialize_response` calls the default FastAPI [jsonable_encoder](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/20d48345465e5b09756cc9ef62fb940c63255a13/fastapi/routing.py#L138) meaning `response_data` has been encoded before the `actual_response_class` is invoked. 

It seems like the recommendation in this thread is to explicitly return a custom `Response` inside each route -- that does work, but it would be nice if we could just set a `default_response_class` when initializing `FastAPI` and call it a day.

Would it be possible to either:
- pass `raw_response` as a kwarg to [actual_response_class](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/20d48345465e5b09756cc9ef62fb940c63255a13/fastapi/routing.py#L250)? 
- pass along a custom json encoder that would be called inside `serialize_response`?

comments.author: ntakouris
comments.body: @nguyent +1 on this, I am using mashumaro.

issues.title: Template project with the essential features
issues.body:
### Description

<!-- Replace the content below with your own feature request -->

FastAPI is great, but for people coming from other backend frameworks (and other programming languages) it is hard for them to invest so much time and energy, until they have build their first code skeleton with all the essential features. So my idea is to provide a small example of a setup with just a few essential elements. I know there is this code generation example of FullStack FastAPI with Postgres and Vue, but for someone, who is not an expert in backend technologies with Python, there is a lot baked in there.
We all want FastAPI to be more popular, so my suggestion is to encourage those, who are looking for their first framework stack with a really simple example just containing:
- OAuth2 with Bearer and JWT for login and registration
- CRUD with async communication
- setup for testing, which rebuilds all tables in the test database.
- Optional Dockerfile

### The solution you would like

A simple GitHub project with these features, which runs on Windows (I have friends, who just work with Windows for programming)

comments.author: Mause
comments.body: So a cut-down version of the existing cookie-cutter template? What in that template do you believe is too much and should be removed to create a new template?

comments.author: Koliham
comments.body:
From my estimations about the skillset of some friends, who work as backend developers with Java/Spring for some years and my teaching experience, I would recommend going further and have a code example without coockie-cutter, but just a plain template like this:
https://github.com/cuongld2/rest_api_service_fastapi
Which you can run with "python main.py".
But it should have a better folder structure for scalability.

The longer it takes, until they are able to run the example the higher is the chance, that they reach the point of frustration to say "oh man, I am investing so much time and it is still not working. I could now have copied my old Spring (or any other backend framework) project and have something running".

comments.author: michaeltoohig
jarmos-san
comments.body:
I feel there would be major differences in opinion on this subject.

> From my estimations about the skill set of some friends, who work as backend developers with Java/Spring for some years and my teaching experience, I would recommend going further and have a code example without cookie-cutter, but just a plain template like this:
> https://github.com/cuongld2/rest_api_service_fastapi
> Which you can run with "python main.py".
> But it should have a better folder structure for scalability.
> 
> The longer it takes, until they are able to run the example the higher is the chance, that they reach the point of frustration to say "oh man, I am investing so much time and it is still not working. I could now have copied my old Spring (or any other backend framework) project and have something running".

Kinda agree with the existing Full Stack FastAPI template(s) for some implementations. 

If you're like me who like to keep things to the most minimal as possible, I think [Poetry ](https://python-poetry.org/ )is the best available option here. No need for Dockerfile, no need for frontend frameworks, no unecessary fluffs but keep room to add what's needed later. For people coming from JS/Java-land, Poetry is like `npm` or `maven`.

That said, managing backend projects with Python couldn't be more simplified that ever if not for Poetry.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Yep, what are the *essential features* becomes quite subjective. There are several FastAPI templates out there with different flavors of tools and components.

I would say that the minimum is just the example in the main page, without DB, without users, etc. Because once users are added that's an assumption about the type of app you're building, the same with the DB and the type of DB you use, etc.

So, the current template is just one flavor and one combination of things to use for an app. But the range from there can vary a lot.

Thanks for the discussion here everyone! ☕ 

> Sorry for the long delay! 🙈  I wanted to personally address each issue/PR and they piled up through time, but now I'm checking each one in order.

issues.title: suggest:  about class-based-views
issues.body:
Hi @tiangolo
First of all, I like FASTAPI very much！
However, if there are a lot of resources, building a router is very cumbersome。
like drf:

```
class EmployeeViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
	queryset = Employee.objects.all()
	serializer_class = EmployeeSerializer
        
         def lists:
              **
         def retrive:
               **
         def put:
              **
         def patch:
               **
         def delete:
               **

```

look like me:
```
from typing import Any, Dict, Generic, List, Optional, Type, TypeVar, Union

from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder
from pydantic import BaseModel
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy import func

#from utils.cbv import InferringRouter, cbv
#cbv is a clss-based-view decorator router

from core.crud import CRUDBase
from core.database import get_db


Base = declarative_base()

ModelType = TypeVar("ModelType", bound=Base)
ListSchemaType = TypeVar("ListSchemaType", bound=BaseModel)
CreateSchemaType = TypeVar("CreateSchemaType", bound=BaseModel)
UpdateSchemaType = TypeVar("UpdateSchemaType", bound=BaseModel) 
PatchSchemaType = TypeVar("PatchSchemaType", bound=BaseModel) 



class ViewSets(Generic[ModelType, ListSchemaType, CreateSchemaType, UpdateSchemaType, PatchSchemaType]):

    db: Session = Depends(get_db)
    
    def __init__(self, model: Type[ModelType], ListSchema:Type[ListSchemaType], CreateSchema:Type[CreateSchemaType], UpdateSchema:Type[UpdateSchemaType],PatchSchema:Type[PatchSchemaType] ):
        self.model = model
        self.ListSchema = ListSchema
        self.CreateSchema = CreateSchema
        self.UpdateSchema = UpdateSchema
        self.PatchSchema = PatchSchema
        self.crud = CRUDBase(self.model)
    
 
    def lists(self, db:Session, skip: int = 0,limit: int = 100):
        res = self.crud.get_multi(db, skip=skip, limit=limit)
        return res

    def create(self, db:Session, in_obj: ListSchemaType):
        db_obj = self.crud.get_by_name(db, name=in_obj.name)
        if db_obj:
            raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="name already registered")
        return self.crud.create(db, in_obj)
    
    def get(self, db:Session, id: int):
        db_obj = self.crud.get(db, id)
        if db_obj is None:                
            raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="not found") 
        return db_obj
    
    def delete(self, db:Session,id: int):
        db_obj = self.crud.get(db, id)
        if db_obj is None:                
            raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="not found")
        self.crud.delete(db, id)
        return db_obj
    
    def put(self, db:Session, id: int, obj_in:UpdateSchemaType ):
        db_obj = self.crud.get(db=db, id=id)
        if not db_obj:
            raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="not found")
        g = self.crud.update(db=db, db_obj=db_obj, obj_in=obj_in)
        return g
    
    def patch(self, db:Session, id: int, obj_in:UpdateSchemaType):
        db_obj = self.crud.get(db=db, id=id)
        if not db_obj:
            raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="not found")
        g = self.crud.update(db=db, db_obj=db_obj, obj_in=obj_in)
        return g

    def as_view(self):
        router =  APIRouter()

        @router.get('/', response_model=List[self.ListSchema])
        async def lists(db=self.db,skip: int = 0,limit: int = 100):
            return self.lists(db=db, skip=skip, limit=limit)
        
        @router.post('/', response_model=self.ListSchema)
        async def post(in_obj:self.CreateSchema, db=self.db):
            return self.create(db,in_obj=in_obj)

        @router.get("/{id}", response_model=self.ListSchema)
        async def retrive(db=self.db, id:int=id):
            return self.get(db,id)
        
        @router.delete("/{id}", response_model=self.ListSchema)
        async def delete(db=self.db, id:int=id):
            return self.delete(db,id)
        
        @router.put("/{id}", response_model=self.ListSchema)
        async def put(in_obj:self.UpdateSchema, db=self.db, id:int=id):
            return self.put(db=db,id=id, obj_in=in_obj)
        
        @router.patch("/{id}", response_model=self.ListSchema)
        async def patch(in_obj:self.PatchSchema, db=self.db, id:int=id):
            return self.patch(db=db,id=id, obj_in=in_obj)
        
        return router
```

usage:
```
userview = ViewSets(models.User, schemas.User, schemas.UserCreate, schemas.UserUpdate, schemas.UserPatch)
app.include_router(userview.as_view(), prefix="/api/users")
	 
```

but looks  very strange,
Are there plans to make class-based-views, like  drf  viewsets  router?

comments.author: erny
comments.body:
Is there a way to change the title to "Generic class based views" ? It's really about generic classes. The problem seems to be that fastapi / pydantic is not able to deduce the correct final classes for function signatures. It seems also that the concrete classes gotten from the generic class, has no `__annotations__` attribute.

I have the same problem:
* python 3.8.5
* fastapi 0.62.
* pydantic 1.7.3

Here goes a minimal test file:
```python
from typing import Generic, Optional, Type, TypeVar

from pydantic import BaseModel
from fastapi import FastAPI, Body, Request
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

T = TypeVar('T', bound=BaseModel)

app = FastAPI()


class GenericViews(Generic[T]):
    _type: T

    def __init__(self, path: str, response_model: Optional[Type[BaseModel]] = None):
        self.path = path
        # delay registering paths until instantiated
        app.add_api_route(
            path=path,
            endpoint=self.echo,
            response_model=response_model,
            methods=['post'],
        )

    async def echo(self, request: Request, x: T = Body(...)) -> T:
        # we get a BaseModel here, instead of Message
        return x


class Message(BaseModel):
    msg: str


MessageViews = GenericViews[Message]
my_generic_views = MessageViews("/messages/hello", response_model=Message)

# Testing code
client = TestClient(app)
# should work, but fails with error in responses (missing field msg). This is because x will be a BaseModel with no attributes.
test_msg = {"msg": "Hello!"}
response = client.post(my_generic_views.path, json=test_msg)
assert response.status_code == 200
assert response.json() == test_msg
# should fail with 422 but does not
test_msg = {"invalid": "invalid!"}
response = client.post(my_generic_views.path, json=test_msg)
assert response.status_code == 422
```
Note: classes derived from generic classes do not have __annotations__ nor can I find a attribute in the instance which gives us the type used for instantiating the generic class.

issues.title: Websockets closing early on ARM
issues.body:
### First check

* [x] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
* [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
* [x] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
* [x] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
* [x] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
* [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).
* [x] After submitting this, I commit to one of:
    * Read open issues with questions until I find 2 issues where I can help someone and add a comment to help there.
    * I already hit the "watch" button in this repository to receive notifications and I commit to help at least 2 people that ask questions in the future.
    * Implement a Pull Request for a confirmed bug.
### Example
This is as close to minimal as I can get. I'm still a bit new to FastAPI so it's not good and I doubt it will work.

The following code runs fine on x86 but on arm64 devices I'm getting the following error:
```
WebSocket is already in CLOSING or CLOSED state.
```
More detailed logs as well as source code available here: 
logs: https://github.com/SelfhostedPro/Yacht/issues/115
SourceCode: https://github.com/SelfhostedPro/Yacht/blob/master/backend/api/routers/apps.py

```Python
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, HTTPException, WebSocket, status, Cookie

import aiodocker
import docker as sdocker

@router.websocket("/stats")
async def dashboard(websocket: WebSocket):
    auth_success = await websocket_auth(websocket=websocket)
    if auth_success:
        await websocket.accept()
        tasks = []
        async with aiodocker.Docker() as docker: 
            containers = []
            _containers = await docker.containers.list()
            for _app in _containers: 
                if _app._container['State'] == 'running':
                    containers.append(_app)
            for app in containers:
                _name = app._container['Names'][0][1:]
                container: DockerContainer = await docker.containers.get(_name)
                stats = container.stats(stream=True)
                tasks.append(process_container(_name, stats, websocket))
            await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    else:
        await websocket.close(code=status.WS_1008_POLICY_VIOLATION)

async def process_container(name, stats, websocket):
    cpu_total = 0.0
    cpu_system = 0.0
    cpu_percent = 0.0
    async for line in stats:
        mem_current = line["memory_stats"]["usage"]
        mem_total = line["memory_stats"]["limit"]

        try:
            cpu_percent, cpu_system, cpu_total = await calculate_cpu_percent2(line, cpu_total, cpu_system)
        except KeyError as e:
            print("error while getting new CPU stats: %r, falling back")
            cpu_percent = await calculate_cpu_percent(line)

        full_stats = {
            "name": name,
            "time": line['read'],
            "cpu_percent": cpu_percent,
            "mem_current": mem_current,
            "mem_total": line["memory_stats"]["limit"],
            "mem_percent": (mem_current / mem_total) * 100.0,
        }
        try:
            await websocket.send_text(json.dumps(full_stats))
        except Exception as e:
            pass
```
### Description
In this code I'm getting stats from docker-py about containers running on the host system. In here there's some calculation to get things going which will sometimes report this error but it shouldn't close the websocket (doesn't on x86 devices).


### Environment
The fastapi version is `fastapi==0.60.2`
The frontend is VueJS
OS: Linux (arm64)
Python: Python 3.8.5

### Additional Context

On x86 I'll sometimes run into this error when a container is in a failing state but it will still keep the websocket open and send data as needed for the other containers. I'm not sure if I'm doing things the wrong way or if there's an underlying issue with websockets on arm devices.

I would like to exclude the ValidationError model from the OpenAPI Schema because I have completely overwritten it like this
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

comments.author: tacozMacleo
comments.body:
Normally you raise it, or make a try-except to get hold of it.
Maybe your should add a Minimal working example on what it is you want! or a longer explanation. 
But in case you just want to use the same ValidationError. From the file: `fastapi/exceptions.py` it look like he is using the `ValidationError` from `pydantic`, you can an example on how to use it here: [Validation Usage](https://pydantic-docs.helpmanual.io/usage/validators/).
Here are the example from the site:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError, validator


class UserModel(BaseModel):
    name: str
    username: str
    password1: str
    password2: str

    @validator('name')
    def name_must_contain_space(cls, v):
        if ' ' not in v:
            raise ValueError('must contain a space')
        return v.title()

    @validator('password2')
    def passwords_match(cls, v, values, **kwargs):
        if 'password1' in values and v != values['password1']:
            raise ValueError('passwords do not match')
        return v

    @validator('username')
    def username_alphanumeric(cls, v):
        assert v.isalnum(), 'must be alphanumeric'
        return v


user = UserModel(
    name='samuel colvin',
    username='scolvin',
    password1='zxcvbn',
    password2='zxcvbn',
)
print(user)
#> name='Samuel Colvin' username='scolvin' password1='zxcvbn' password2='zxcvbn'

try:
    UserModel(
        name='samuel',
        username='scolvin',
        password1='zxcvbn',
        password2='zxcvbn2',
    )
except ValidationError as e:
    print(e)
    """
    2 validation errors for UserModel
    name
      must contain a space (type=value_error)
    password2
      passwords do not match (type=value_error)
    """

```
